kunyomi: お.す, へ.す, おさ.える
fact: Japan is famous for high-pressure situations like crowded commuter trains during rush hour. The word 圧力 (atsuryoku), meaning 'pressure,' is commonly used in science, engineering, and everyday conversation.
memory: Imagine a heavy cliff (厂) pressing down on the earth (土). The land is being squeezed under pressure, helping you remember 圧 means 'pressure' or 'to compress.'

sentence.japanese: 今日は 気圧 が 低いです。
sentence.reading: きょうは きあつ が ひくいです。
sentence.romaji: kyou wa kiatsu ga hikui desu
sentence.english: The atmospheric pressure is low today.

fact: Japanese workplaces often emphasize teamwork. The verb 依頼する (irai suru), meaning 'to request,' comes from this kanji because making a request means relying on someone else.
memory: A person (亻) leans against their clothes (衣) for comfort. They are relying on support, helping you remember 依 means 'depend' or 'rely.'

sentence.japanese: 友達 に 依頼しました。
sentence.reading: ともだち に いらいしました。
sentence.romaji: tomodachi ni irai shimashita
sentence.english: I made a request to my friend.

fact: The traditional Japanese board game 囲碁 (Igo) gets its name from this kanji because players surround their opponent's stones to capture territory.
memory: Imagine building a fence around a field to protect it. The outer box completely surrounds everything inside, helping you remember 囲 means 'to surround.'

sentence.japanese: 子どもたち が 先生 を 囲みました。
sentence.reading: こどもたち が せんせい を かこみました。
sentence.romaji: kodomotachi ga sensei o kakomimashita
sentence.english: The children surrounded the teacher.

fact: Japanese schools and companies often have many committees called 委員会 (iinkai). Students may serve on the library committee, festival committee, or sports committee.
memory: Imagine handing important rice plants (禾) to a woman (女) because you trust her to take care of them. You are entrusting responsibility, which is the meaning of 委.

sentence.japanese: 私は 委員会 に 入っています。
sentence.reading: わたしは いいんかい に はいっています。
sentence.romaji: watashi wa iinkai ni haitteimasu
sentence.english: I belong to a committee.

fact: Japan's railway stations frequently display the word 乗り換え and 案内 for transfers, while 移動 (idou) is commonly used to mean moving from one place to another.
memory: Imagine carrying harvested grain (禾) from one place to another many (多) times. You're constantly moving it, making 移 easy to remember as 'move' or 'transfer.'

sentence.japanese: 荷物 を 車 に 移しました。
sentence.reading: にもつ を くるま に うつしました。
sentence.romaji: nimotsu o kuruma ni utsushimashita
sentence.english: I moved the luggage into the car.

fact: Japanese pharmacies and convenience stores commonly sell 胃薬 (igusuri), or stomach medicine, because the kanji 胃 appears in many health-related products and advertisements.
memory: Imagine your body (月) holding a field (田) full of food after a big meal. That field stores everything you eat, reminding you that 胃 means 'stomach.'

sentence.japanese: 昨日 食べすぎて 胃 が 痛いです。
sentence.reading: きのう たべすぎて い が いたいです。
sentence.romaji: kinou tabesugite i ga itai desu
sentence.english: I ate too much yesterday, so my stomach hurts.

fact: Traditional Japanese clothing like the kimono is often described using the kanji 衣, which also appears in words related to clothing and costumes.
memory: The flowing shape of 衣 looks like a robe with wide sleeves hanging from a person's shoulders. Picture someone wearing beautiful traditional clothes.

sentence.japanese: 新しい 衣服 を 買いました。
sentence.reading: あたらしい いふく を かいました。
sentence.romaji: atarashii ifuku o kaimashita
sentence.english: I bought new clothes.

fact: The word 地域 (chiiki), meaning 'region' or 'local area,' is frequently used in Japan for community events, local governments, and disaster preparedness programs.
memory: Imagine a piece of land (土) protected by strong borders. Everything inside that boundary belongs to one region or area, helping you remember 域.

sentence.japanese: この 地域 は とても 静かです。
sentence.reading: この ちいき は とても しずかです。
sentence.romaji: kono chiiki wa totemo shizuka desu
sentence.english: This area is very quiet.

fact: Instead of signatures, many Japanese people use a personal seal called a hanko or inkan. The kanji 印 means 'seal' and appears in words like 印鑑 (inkan).
memory: Imagine stamping an official mark onto a document. That single stamp leaves a permanent 印, meaning a mark or seal.

sentence.japanese: ここに 印 を 押してください。
sentence.reading: ここに しるし を おしてください。
sentence.romaji: koko ni shirushi o oshite kudasai
sentence.english: Please put your mark here.

fact: The word 宇宙 (uchuu), meaning 'universe' or 'outer space,' uses this kanji. Japan's space agency and science museums frequently use this word when talking about astronomy.
memory: Imagine standing under the roof (宀) and looking upward into the endless universe. The roof protects you while the vast space above reminds you of 宇.

sentence.japanese: 子ども は 宇宙 に 興味 が あります。
sentence.reading: こども は うちゅう に きょうみ が あります。
sentence.romaji: kodomo wa uchuu ni kyoumi ga arimasu
sentence.english: The child is interested in outer space.

fact: In Japan, the graceful crane is a symbol of longevity and good fortune. Its beautiful 羽 (feathers) inspired traditional arts such as origami, where folding a thousand paper cranes is believed to bring good luck.
memory: The two sides of 羽 look like a pair of bird wings spread open. Picture a bird flapping its wings and soaring into the sky.

sentence.japanese: 鳥 が 羽 を 広げました。
sentence.reading: とり が は を ひろげました。
sentence.romaji: tori ga ha o hirogemashita
sentence.english: The bird spread its wings.

fact: Mount Fuji is famous for the 'sea of clouds' (雲海, unkai), where early morning clouds cover the landscape like an ocean. Many photographers travel across Japan just to capture this breathtaking view.
memory: Rain (雨) gathers high in the sky and forms fluffy clouds. Imagine the top of the kanji filling with rain before becoming a cloud.

sentence.japanese: 空 に 白い 雲 が 浮かんでいます。
sentence.reading: そら に しろい くも が うかんでいます。
sentence.romaji: sora ni shiroi kumo ga ukandeimasu
sentence.english: White clouds are floating in the sky.

fact: Many family-owned businesses in Japan proudly display 営業中 (eigyouchuu), meaning 'Open for Business,' outside their shops every morning.
memory: Imagine many people working together inside a building from morning until night. Running a business takes constant effort, helping you remember 営 means 'operate' or 'manage.'

sentence.japanese: 父 は レストラン を 営んでいます。
sentence.reading: ちち は レストラン を いとなんでいます。
sentence.romaji: chichi wa resutoran o itonandeimasu
sentence.english: My father runs a restaurant.

fact: Nagoya's famous shopping and entertainment district is called 栄 (Sakae), a name chosen because the area was expected to prosper and flourish.
memory: Imagine a healthy tree (木) growing bigger every year until it becomes strong and prosperous. A flourishing tree reminds you of 栄.

sentence.japanese: 町 は 大きく 栄えました。
sentence.reading: まち は おおきく さかえました。
sentence.romaji: machi wa ookiku sakaemashita
sentence.english: The town prospered greatly.

fact: The famous Japanese phrase 永遠 (eien) means 'eternity' and appears in songs, anime, literature, and even wedding ceremonies to express everlasting love or friendship.
memory: Picture a river flowing endlessly without ever stopping. Like flowing water, 永 represents something that lasts forever.

sentence.japanese: この 思い出 は 永遠 に 忘れません。
sentence.reading: この おもいで は えいえん に わすれません。
sentence.romaji: kono omoide wa eien ni wasuremasen
sentence.english: I will never forget this memory.

fact: Traditional Japanese swordsmiths spent months forging a single katana. A truly 鋭い (surudoi) blade was prized not only for its sharpness but also as a symbol of craftsmanship and honor.
memory: Imagine polishing a metal (金) sword until it becomes so sharp that it can slice through anything. The shining metal reminds you that 鋭 means 'sharp' or 'keen.'

sentence.japanese: 彼 は 鋭い 観察力 を 持っています。
sentence.reading: かれ は するどい かんさつりょく を もっています。
sentence.romaji: kare wa surudoi kansatsuryoku o motteimasu
sentence.english: He has keen observation skills.

fact: Japan is famous for its high-tech vending machines that sell everything from bottled drinks to hot soup. Many products are described as 液体 (ekitai), meaning 'liquid.'
memory: The water radical (氵) tells you it's something that flows. Imagine water turning into different liquids like juice, medicine, or fuel, helping you remember 液.

sentence.japanese: この 液体 は 飲めません。
sentence.reading: この えきたい は のめません。
sentence.romaji: kono ekitai wa nomemasen
sentence.english: This liquid is not drinkable.

kunyomi: の.びる, の.べる, の.ばす
fact: Japan's famous Shinkansen occasionally announces delays using the word 延着 (enchaku), meaning 'late arrival,' which comes from the idea of extending travel time.
memory: Picture a long road stretching farther and farther into the distance. It keeps extending, helping you remember 延 means 'to extend' or 'to prolong.'

sentence.japanese: 会議 は 一時間 延長 されました。
sentence.reading: かいぎ は いちじかん えんちょう されました。
sentence.romaji: kaigi wa ichijikan enchou saremashita
sentence.english: The meeting was extended by one hour.

fact: Before entering a sumo ring, wrestlers throw 塩 (salt) to purify the ring according to Shinto tradition. This ritual has been practiced in Japan for centuries.
memory: Imagine white salt piled high on the ground (土). The little mountain of salt reminds you that 塩 means 'salt.'

sentence.japanese: 料理 に 少し 塩 を 入れます。
sentence.reading: りょうり に すこし しお を いれます。
sentence.romaji: ryouri ni sukoshi shio o iremasu
sentence.english: I add a little salt to the dish.

kunyomi: よご.す, よご.れる, けが.す, けが.れる
fact: Japan is known for its remarkably clean streets. School children often clean their own classrooms every day, reflecting the cultural value of avoiding 汚れ (yogore), or dirt and mess.
memory: The water radical (氵) shows something involving water. Imagine clean water becoming muddy and dirty, making it easy to remember 汚 means 'dirty' or 'to pollute.'

sentence.japanese: 服 が 汚れて しまいました。
sentence.reading: ふく が よごれて しまいました。
sentence.romaji: fuku ga yogorete shimaimashita
sentence.english: My clothes became dirty.

fact: Tokyo's famous district 中央区 (Chūō-ku) literally means 'Central Ward.' The kanji 央 often appears in place names and organizations to indicate the center of an area.
memory: Imagine a person standing exactly in the middle of a large square. Everyone gathers around that person because they are at the center. That image helps you remember 央 means 'center.'

sentence.japanese: 駅 の 中央 に 時計 が あります。
sentence.reading: えき の ちゅうおう に とけい が あります。
sentence.romaji: eki no chuuou ni tokei ga arimasu
sentence.english: There is a clock in the center of the station.

fact: Many traditional Japanese homes have an 奥の間 (oku no ma), the innermost room reserved for honored guests or important family occasions.
memory: Imagine walking through a house until you reach the deepest room at the very back. That hidden place is the 奥, the innermost part.

sentence.japanese: 本 は 部屋 の 奥 に あります。
sentence.reading: ほん は へや の おく に あります。
sentence.romaji: hon wa heya no oku ni arimasu
sentence.english: The book is in the back of the room.

fact: The kanji 欧 is almost exclusively used in words related to Europe. For example, 欧州 (Ōshū) means 'Europe,' while 欧米 (Ōbei) refers to Europe and America together.
memory: Think of Europe far across the ocean from Japan. The kanji 欧 is your shortcut for anything related to Europe.

sentence.japanese: 彼 は 欧州 へ 出張しました。
sentence.reading: かれ は おうしゅう へ しゅっちょうしました。
sentence.romaji: kare wa oushuu e shucchou shimashita
sentence.english: He went on a business trip to Europe.

fact: Every autumn, Japan's ginkgo trees turn a brilliant yellow. Places like Meiji Jingu Gaien become famous for their golden 黄 leaves and attract thousands of visitors.
memory: Imagine bright yellow leaves covering the ground in autumn. The glowing golden color helps you remember 黄 means 'yellow.'

sentence.japanese: 黄色 の 花 が 咲いています。
sentence.reading: きいろ の はな が さいています。
sentence.romaji: kiiro no hana ga saiteimasu
sentence.english: Yellow flowers are blooming.

fact: Japan uses 億 (100,000,000) instead of 'hundred million' in everyday life. For example, Tokyo's population is often expressed using 億 and 万 rather than millions.
memory: Imagine stacking so much money that counting millions isn't enough—you need a much bigger unit. That enormous number is 億, meaning one hundred million.

sentence.japanese: その 会社 は 一億 円 を 投資しました。
sentence.reading: その かいしゃ は いちおく えん を とうししました。
sentence.romaji: sono kaisha wa ichioku en o toushi shimashita
sentence.english: The company invested one hundred million yen.

kunyomi: あたた.かい, あたた.まる, あたた.める
fact: Japan is famous for its 温泉 (onsen), natural hot springs heated by volcanic activity. Visiting an onsen is one of the country's most beloved traditions.
memory: Imagine warm water (氵) filling a bath under the warm sun. The steaming water reminds you that 温 means 'warm' or 'temperature.'

sentence.japanese: 今日は 気温 が 高いです。
sentence.reading: きょう は きおん が たかいです。
sentence.romaji: kyou wa kion ga takai desu
sentence.english: The temperature is high today.

fact: The Sumida River (隅田川) in Tokyo hosts one of Japan's largest summer fireworks festivals. Large rivers have played an important role in Japanese transportation and culture for centuries.
memory: The water radical (氵) tells you it has something to do with water. Imagine a wide river flowing endlessly toward the sea.

sentence.japanese: 大きな 河 を 渡りました。
sentence.reading: おおきな かわ を わたりました。
sentence.romaji: ookina kawa o watarimashita
sentence.english: We crossed a large river.

fact: Japan's famous luggage forwarding service, 宅急便 (Takkyūbin), allows travelers to send their 荷物 (nimotsu) directly to hotels, making train travel much easier.
memory: Imagine carrying heavy luggage made of plants (艹) stacked on your shoulders. The heavy load reminds you that 荷 means 'baggage' or 'load.'

sentence.japanese: 荷物 を 車 に 積みました。
sentence.reading: にもつ を くるま に つみました。
sentence.romaji: nimotsu o kuruma ni tsumimashita
sentence.english: I loaded the luggage into the car.

fact: Traditional Japanese sweets, called 和菓子 (wagashi), are carefully crafted to reflect the seasons and are often served during tea ceremonies.
memory: Imagine delicious sweets growing like fruit under the grass (艹). Whenever you see this kanji, think of colorful Japanese desserts.

sentence.japanese: 和菓子 は お茶 に よく 合います。
sentence.reading: わがし は おちゃ に よく あいます。
sentence.romaji: wagashi wa ocha ni yoku aimasu
sentence.english: Japanese sweets go well with tea.

fact: Japanese companies are divided into departments called 課 (ka), such as 人事課 (Human Resources Section). The kanji also appears in textbook lessons like 第一課 (Lesson 1).
memory: Imagine receiving today's assignment from your teacher before class begins. Every lesson comes with a new 課, helping you remember 'lesson' or 'assignment.'

sentence.japanese: 今日 の 課題 は 難しかったです。
sentence.reading: きょう の かだい は むずかしかったです。
sentence.romaji: kyou no kadai wa muzukashikatta desu
sentence.english: Today's assignment was difficult.

fact: Japan's money is called 通貨 (tsūka), meaning 'currency.' The kanji 貨 appears in words related to trade, shopping, and finance, making it common in banks and business news.
memory: Imagine valuable shells and coins piled together as trade goods. Whenever merchants exchange products or money, think of 貨 meaning 'goods' or 'currency.'

sentence.japanese: 外国 の 通貨 を 両替しました。
sentence.reading: がいこく の つうか を りょうがえしました。
sentence.romaji: gaikoku no tsuuka o ryougae shimashita
sentence.english: I exchanged foreign currency.

fact: In Japan, introducing two people in business is called 紹介 (shōkai). Proper introductions are considered an important part of Japanese etiquette and networking.
memory: Imagine a person standing between two friends and introducing them to each other. Acting as the middle person helps you remember 介 means 'mediate' or 'assist.'

sentence.japanese: 友達 が 先生 を 紹介してくれました。
sentence.reading: ともだち が せんせい を しょうかいしてくれました。
sentence.romaji: tomodachi ga sensei o shoukai shite kuremashita
sentence.english: My friend introduced me to the teacher.

fact: The famous Japanese train service 快速 (kaisoku) means 'Rapid Service.' The idea is that a fast and smooth journey is more pleasant for passengers.
memory: Imagine your heart (忄) feeling light and refreshed after a cool breeze. A happy heart reminds you that 快 means 'pleasant' or 'comfortable.'

sentence.japanese: この 部屋 は とても 快適です。
sentence.reading: この へや は とても かいてきです。
sentence.romaji: kono heya wa totemo kaiteki desu
sentence.english: This room is very comfortable.

kunyomi: あらた.める, あらた.まる
fact: Japan has undergone several major reforms called 改革 (kaikaku), from the Meiji Restoration to modern government reforms. The kanji 改 often represents positive change or improvement.
memory: Imagine using your hand to fix and improve something broken. Every repair makes it better, helping you remember 改 means 'change' or 'improve.'

sentence.japanese: 生活 を 改善したいです。
sentence.reading: せいかつ を かいぜんしたいです。
sentence.romaji: seikatsu o kaizen shitai desu
sentence.english: I want to improve my lifestyle.

fact: Japan is one of the world's leading manufacturers of industrial machinery and robots. The kanji 械 appears in words related to machines, engineering, and automation.
memory: Imagine a huge wooden framework (木) holding together a powerful machine. That machine reminds you that 械 means 'machinery.'

sentence.japanese: この 工場 は 新しい 機械 を 使っています。
sentence.reading: この こうじょう は あたらしい きかい を つかっています。
sentence.romaji: kono koujou wa atarashii kikai o tsukatteimasu
sentence.english: This factory uses new machines.

fact: During traditional Japanese festivals and tea ceremonies, charcoal is burned to heat water. After the fire goes out, only 灰 (ash) remains, and in some tea schools the ash is arranged artistically.
memory: Imagine a fire (火) burning under a cliff (厂). When the flames die out, only gray ash is left. That leftover ash is 灰.

sentence.japanese: 火 が 消えて 灰 だけ 残りました。
sentence.reading: ひ が きえて はい だけ のこりました。
sentence.romaji: hi ga kiete hai dake nokorimashita
sentence.english: The fire went out, leaving only ash.

fact: In Japan, department stores often have detailed floor guides labeled 一階, 二階, 三階, making 階 one of the first kanji visitors learn while shopping.
memory: Imagine climbing one staircase after another until you reach a higher floor. Every new level is another 階.

sentence.japanese: 私 の 部屋 は 二階 に あります。
sentence.reading: わたし の へや は にかい に あります。
sentence.romaji: watashi no heya wa nikai ni arimasu
sentence.english: My room is on the second floor.

fact: Japanese train stations often display 各駅停車 (kakueki teisha), meaning 'local train,' because it stops at each station instead of skipping stations.
memory: Imagine handing one gift to every person in a line. Since everyone receives one, think of 各 as meaning 'each' or 'every.'

sentence.japanese: 各 グループ が 発表しました。
sentence.reading: かく グループ が はっぴょうしました。
sentence.romaji: kaku guruupu ga happyou shimashita
sentence.english: Each group gave a presentation.

fact: Japanese city maps often use the word 角 (kado) when giving directions, such as 'turn at the next corner,' making this a very practical kanji for travelers.
memory: Picture the sharp corner of a building or the pointed horn of a deer. Both have a clear angle, helping you remember 角.

sentence.japanese: 次 の 角 を 右 に 曲がってください。
sentence.reading: つぎ の かど を みぎ に まがってください。
sentence.romaji: tsugi no kado o migi ni magatte kudasai
sentence.english: Please turn right at the next corner.

fact: The word 革命 (kakumei), meaning 'revolution,' is widely used in Japan for major technological breakthroughs, while 革製品 refers to leather products such as wallets and bags.
memory: Imagine a piece of animal skin being carefully stretched and treated until it becomes strong leather. That finished leather is 革.

sentence.japanese: この 財布 は 革 で 作られています。
sentence.reading: この さいふ は かわ で つくられています。
sentence.romaji: kono saifu wa kawa de tsukurareteimasu
sentence.english: This wallet is made of leather.

fact: When shopping in Japan, receipts often show 合計額 (goukeigaku), meaning 'total amount.' You'll frequently see this kanji in stores, banks, and online shopping websites.
memory: Imagine someone with a price tag stuck on their forehead. Since 額 also means 'forehead' and is often used for an 'amount of money,' the unusual image makes both meanings memorable.

sentence.japanese: 請求 の 総額 は 一万円です。
sentence.reading: せいきゅう の そうがく は いちまんえんです。
sentence.romaji: seikyuu no sougaku wa ichiman en desu
sentence.english: The total bill is 10,000 yen.

fact: Japan's rainy season is followed by many sunny days, and laundry hanging outside to 乾く (kawaku), or dry, is a common sight across the country.
memory: Imagine wet clothes hanging under the bright sun until every drop of water disappears. That's exactly what 乾 means—to dry.

sentence.japanese: 洗濯物 が もう 乾きました。
sentence.reading: せんたくもの が もう かわきました。
sentence.romaji: sentakumono ga mou kawakimashita
sentence.english: The laundry has already dried.

fact: Japanese magazines often include the word 月刊 (gekkan), meaning 'monthly publication.' Similar words include 週刊 (weekly) and 年刊 (yearly).
memory: Imagine a freshly printed magazine coming off a printing press. Every new issue is another publication, helping you remember 刊 means 'publish.'

sentence.japanese: この 雑誌 は 月刊です。
sentence.reading: この ざっし は げっかんです。
sentence.romaji: kono zasshi wa gekkan desu
sentence.english: This magazine is published monthly.

fact: Japanese manga volumes are numbered using 巻. For example, 第十巻 means 'Volume 10,' making this kanji familiar to manga readers worldwide.
memory: Imagine rolling up a long scroll into a neat cylinder. That rolled-up scroll is a 巻, helping you remember both 'roll' and 'volume.'

sentence.japanese: 新しい 漫画 の 第一巻 を 買いました。
sentence.reading: あたらしい まんが の だいいっかん を かいました。
sentence.romaji: atarashii manga no daiikkan o kaimashita
sentence.english: I bought the first volume of a new manga.

kunyomi: ほ.す, ひ.る, ほ.し
fact: Traditional Japanese foods like dried fish (干物, himono) are made by hanging them in the sun. The kanji 干 originally referred to drying things outdoors.
memory: Picture clothes hanging on a simple pole under the hot sun. The pole is used to dry everything, helping you remember 干 means 'to dry.'

sentence.japanese: 外 に 洗濯物 を 干しました。
sentence.reading: そと に せんたくもの を ほしました。
sentence.romaji: soto ni sentakumono o hoshimashita
sentence.english: I hung the laundry outside to dry.

fact: Japanese hospitals often use the word 患者 (kanja), meaning 'patient.' You'll frequently see this kanji on hospital signs, medical forms, and healthcare documents.
memory: Imagine your heart (心) hurting because you're sick. A person with a troubled heart is suffering from an illness, helping you remember 患 means 'to suffer' or 'illness.'

sentence.japanese: 祖父 は 長い間 病気 を 患っていました。
sentence.reading: そふ は ながいあいだ びょうき を わずらっていました。
sentence.romaji: sofu wa nagai aida byouki o wazuratteimashita
sentence.english: My grandfather suffered from an illness for a long time.

fact: Foreign visitors to Japan often see the word 両替 (ryōgae), but banks also use 交換 (kōkan) and 交換所 when referring to exchanges and exchange counters.
memory: Imagine handing one item to a friend while receiving another in return. Swapping one thing for another helps you remember 換 means 'exchange' or 'replace.'

sentence.japanese: 新しい 電池 に 交換しました。
sentence.reading: あたらしい でんち に こうかんしました。
sentence.romaji: atarashii denchi ni koukan shimashita
sentence.english: I replaced it with a new battery.

fact: Japan's hot and humid summers are famous for making people sweat. Cooling towels and portable fans are popular items sold every summer to deal with 汗.
memory: The water radical (氵) shows something liquid. Imagine drops of water running down your face after exercising—they're drops of sweat.

sentence.japanese: 暑くて 汗 が 止まりません。
sentence.reading: あつくて あせ が とまりません。
sentence.romaji: atsukute ase ga tomarimasen
sentence.english: It's so hot that I can't stop sweating.

kunyomi: あま.い, あま.える, あま.やかす
fact: Japanese desserts such as wagashi are known for their gentle sweetness. The word 甘味 (kanmi) refers to sweet flavors and traditional sweet shops are called 甘味処.
memory: Imagine tasting a delicious piece of candy. The moment it touches your tongue, you smile because it's wonderfully sweet. That feeling is 甘.

sentence.japanese: この ケーキ は とても 甘いです。
sentence.reading: この ケーキ は とても あまいです。
sentence.romaji: kono keeki wa totemo amai desu
sentence.english: This cake is very sweet.

fact: The word 管理 (kanri), meaning 'management,' is one of the most common business terms in Japanese. You'll see it in words like 管理者 (administrator) and 管理室 (control room).
memory: Imagine a long bamboo tube carrying water through a garden. Since tubes help control where water goes, 管 also came to mean 'manage.'

sentence.japanese: 彼 は この 建物 を 管理しています。
sentence.reading: かれ は この たてもの を かんりしています。
sentence.romaji: kare wa kono tatemono o kanri shiteimasu
sentence.english: He manages this building.

fact: Before paper became common in Japan, important writings were recorded on bamboo slips. These bamboo strips were called 簡 (kan), and because messages had to fit on a small strip, the kanji came to mean 'simple' or 'brief.'
memory: Imagine writing a message on a single bamboo strip (⺮). Since there isn't much space, you keep your message short and simple. That is the meaning of 簡.

sentence.japanese: 説明 を 簡単 に してください。
sentence.reading: せつめい を かんたん に してください。
sentence.romaji: setsumei o kantan ni shite kudasai
sentence.english: Please explain it simply.

fact: Japan has one of the highest numbers of vending machines in the world. Many sell 缶コーヒー (canned coffee), a uniquely popular drink enjoyed by commuters every day.
memory: Picture a shiny aluminum can sitting inside a vending machine. Every time you press the button, out pops a 缶.

sentence.japanese: 缶コーヒー を 買いました。
sentence.reading: かんコーヒー を かいました。
sentence.romaji: kan koohii o kaimashita
sentence.english: I bought a can of coffee.

kunyomi: まる, まる.い, まる.める
fact: Many Japanese ships traditionally end their names with 丸 (maru), such as 日本丸. One theory says it symbolizes completeness and good fortune during voyages.
memory: Imagine drawing a perfect circle with one smooth motion. That round shape reminds you that 丸 means 'round' or 'circle.'

sentence.japanese: この テーブル は 丸いです。
sentence.reading: この テーブル は まるいです。
sentence.romaji: kono teeburu wa marui desu
sentence.english: This table is round.

fact: Food labels in Japan often use the word 含む (fukumu) to indicate ingredients contained in a product, especially allergy information.
memory: Imagine opening your mouth (口) to hold something inside. Whatever is inside is being contained, helping you remember 含 means 'contain' or 'include.'

sentence.japanese: この 飲み物 は 牛乳 を 含んでいます。
sentence.reading: この のみもの は ぎゅうにゅう を ふくんでいます。
sentence.romaji: kono nomimono wa gyuunyuu o fukundeimasu
sentence.english: This drink contains milk.

fact: Japan's coastline is famous for dramatic rock formations such as the Meoto Iwa ('Wedded Rocks') in Mie Prefecture, where two sacred rocks are connected by a giant rope.
memory: Imagine a huge rock (石) standing on top of a mountain (山). Together they form a massive boulder, reminding you that 岩 means 'rock.'

sentence.japanese: 大きな 岩 の 上 に 座りました。
sentence.reading: おおきな いわ の うえ に すわりました。
sentence.romaji: ookina iwa no ue ni suwarimashita
sentence.english: I sat on a large rock.

fact: The word 希望 (kibou), meaning 'hope,' is commonly heard during graduation ceremonies in Japan, where students are encouraged to pursue their dreams with hope for the future.
memory: Imagine reaching high toward a distant star because you hope to touch it someday. That distant dream represents 希, meaning 'hope' or 'wish.'

sentence.japanese: 希望 を 持って 頑張ってください。
sentence.reading: きぼう を もって がんばってください。
sentence.romaji: kibou o motte ganbatte kudasai
sentence.english: Please keep hope and do your best.

fact: Japanese students are responsible for cleaning their own classrooms, including wiping their 机 (desks), as part of daily school life.
memory: Imagine opening your notebook on a wooden desk every morning before class begins. That study desk is 机.

sentence.japanese: 本 は 机 の 上 に あります。
sentence.reading: ほん は つくえ の うえ に あります。
sentence.romaji: hon wa tsukue no ue ni arimasu
sentence.english: The book is on the desk.

fact: Japan celebrates the changing of the four seasons through festivals, seasonal foods, and traditions. The word 四季 (shiki), meaning 'the four seasons,' is deeply connected to Japanese culture.
memory: Imagine a young plant growing differently in every season. Watching it change through spring, summer, autumn, and winter helps you remember 季 means 'season.'

sentence.japanese: 日本 は 四季 が はっきりしています。
sentence.reading: にほん は しき が はっきりしています。
sentence.romaji: nihon wa shiki ga hakkiri shiteimasu
sentence.english: Japan has four distinct seasons.

fact: Martial arts such as judo, karate, and kendo place great importance on mastering 技 (waza), the techniques passed down from teacher to student over generations.
memory: Imagine a skilled craftsman using his hand (扌) to perform an amazing technique. His practiced hand reminds you that 技 means 'skill' or 'technique.'

sentence.japanese: 彼 は 高い 技術 を 持っています。
sentence.reading: かれ は たかい ぎじゅつ を もっています。
sentence.romaji: kare wa takai gijutsu o motteimasu
sentence.english: He has excellent technical skills.

fact: Japan has designated 喫煙所 (kitsuenjo), or smoking areas, in many train stations and public places. The kanji 喫 appears frequently on signs related to smoking and cafés.
memory: Imagine bringing a cup to your mouth and enjoying a drink. Putting something into your mouth helps you remember 喫 means 'to consume,' especially drinking or smoking.

sentence.japanese: 駅 の 喫煙所 を 利用しました。
sentence.reading: えき の きつえんじょ を りようしました。
sentence.romaji: eki no kitsuenjo o riyou shimashita
sentence.english: I used the smoking area at the station.

kunyomi: つ.める, つ.まる, つ.む
fact: Japanese lunch boxes, called お弁当 (obentō), are carefully packed with colorful food. The verb 詰める (tsumeru) is commonly used when filling a lunch box before school or work.
memory: Imagine stuffing so many books into a backpack that there's no room left. The bag is completely packed, helping you remember 詰 means 'to fill' or 'to pack.'

sentence.japanese: 母 は お弁当 を 詰めています。
sentence.reading: はは は おべんとう を つめています。
sentence.romaji: haha wa obentou o tsumeteimasu
sentence.english: My mother is packing a lunch box.

kunyomi: さか.らう, さか.らい, さか.さ
fact: Japanese roads drive on the left side, so many visitors feel it's the 'opposite' of what they're used to. The word 逆 (gyaku) is often used whenever something is reversed.
memory: Imagine everyone walking in one direction while one person walks the opposite way. That person is going in reverse, reminding you of 逆.

sentence.japanese: 地図 を 逆さ に 持っています。
sentence.reading: ちず を さかさ に もっています。
sentence.romaji: chizu o sakasa ni motteimasu
sentence.english: You are holding the map upside down.

fact: The phrase お久しぶりです (Ohisashiburi desu), meaning 'Long time no see,' is one of the most common greetings in Japanese and comes from this kanji.
memory: Imagine waiting for a friend for such a long time that it feels endless. That long wait helps you remember 久 means 'a long time.'

sentence.japanese: 彼 と 久しぶり に 会いました。
sentence.reading: かれ と ひさしぶり に あいました。
sentence.romaji: kare to hisashiburi ni aimashita
sentence.english: I met him after a long time.

fact: Historic buildings in Japan are often labeled 旧 (kyū), meaning 'former' or 'old.' For example, 旧東京駅 refers to the original Tokyo Station building.
memory: Imagine finding an old photograph from years ago. It reminds you of former times, making 旧 easy to remember as 'old' or 'former.'

sentence.japanese: 旧友 と 再会しました。
sentence.reading: きゅうゆう と さいかいしました。
sentence.romaji: kyuuyuu to saikai shimashita
sentence.english: I reunited with an old friend.

fact: The word 巨人 (Kyojin), meaning 'giant,' is also the name of the famous Yomiuri Giants baseball team, one of Japan's most popular professional sports clubs.
memory: Imagine a giant standing taller than every building around him. His enormous size helps you remember 巨 means 'huge' or 'giant.'

sentence.japanese: 巨大 な ビル が 建っています。
sentence.reading: きょだい な ビル が たっています。
sentence.romaji: kyodai na biru ga tatteimasu
sentence.english: A huge building is standing there.

fact: Japan hosts many traditional competitions, from sumo tournaments to high school sports festivals. The word 競技 (kyōgi), meaning 'competition' or 'sporting event,' is commonly seen during the Olympics and national championships.
memory: Imagine two runners racing side by side toward the finish line. Each one is trying to beat the other, helping you remember that 競 means 'to compete.'

sentence.japanese: 世界中 の 選手 が 金メダル を 競いました。
sentence.reading: せかいじゅう の せんしゅ が きんメダル を きそいました。
sentence.romaji: sekaijuu no senshu ga kin medaru o kisoimashita
sentence.english: Athletes from around the world competed for the gold medal.

fact: Japanese schools and workplaces place great importance on cooperation. The word 協力 (kyōryoku), meaning 'cooperation,' appears frequently on notices asking everyone to work together.
memory: Imagine several people joining hands to lift something too heavy for one person. Working together reminds you that 協 means 'cooperate.'

sentence.japanese: みんな の 協力 が 必要です。
sentence.reading: みんな の きょうりょく が ひつようです。
sentence.romaji: minna no kyouryoku ga hitsuyou desu
sentence.english: Everyone's cooperation is necessary.

fact: At Japanese baseball games, enthusiastic fans loudly cheer and sometimes 叫ぶ (sakebu), or shout, to encourage their favorite team.
memory: Imagine opening your mouth wide and shouting so loudly that everyone hears you. That powerful voice helps you remember 叫 means 'to shout.'

words.reading: ひめい を さけぶ

sentence.japanese: 彼 は 助け を 求めて 叫びました。
sentence.reading: かれ は たすけ を もとめて さけびました。
sentence.romaji: kare wa tasuke o motomete sakebimashita
sentence.english: He shouted for help.

fact: The word 環境 (kankyō), meaning 'environment,' is one of the most common words in Japanese news, especially when discussing climate change and recycling.
memory: Imagine a fence marking where one country ends and another begins. That dividing line is a boundary, helping you remember 境.

sentence.japanese: 自然 環境 を 守りましょう。
sentence.reading: しぜん かんきょう を まもりましょう。
sentence.romaji: shizen kankyou o mamorimashou
sentence.english: Let's protect the natural environment.

fact: Japanese convenience stores sell many foods with fillings sandwiched inside, such as sandwiches and dorayaki. The verb 挟む (hasamu) literally means 'to put something in between.'
memory: Imagine using your hand (扌) to pinch a sheet of paper between two fingers. Holding something tightly in the middle helps you remember 挟 means 'to pinch' or 'to sandwich.'

words.reading: ほん を はさむ
くち を はさむ

sentence.japanese: 指 を ドア に 挟みました。
sentence.reading: ゆび を ドア に はさみました。
sentence.romaji: yubi o doa ni hasamimashita
sentence.english: I caught my finger in the door.

fact: The word 状況 (jōkyō), meaning 'situation,' appears frequently in Japanese news broadcasts, weather reports, and emergency announcements to describe current conditions.
memory: Imagine looking at a river (氵) to check how the water is flowing. The state of the water tells you the current situation, helping you remember 況 means 'condition' or 'situation.'

sentence.japanese: 現在 の 状況 を 説明してください。
sentence.reading: げんざい の じょうきょう を せつめいしてください。
sentence.romaji: genzai no joukyou o setsumei shite kudasai
sentence.english: Please explain the current situation.

fact: The expression 胸がいっぱい (mune ga ippai) literally means 'my chest is full,' but it is used to describe being overwhelmed with emotion, such as joy or gratitude.
memory: Imagine placing your hand over your chest whenever you feel strong emotions. Your chest becomes the place where feelings gather, helping you remember 胸.

words.reading: むね が いっぱい

sentence.japanese: 彼 は 胸 を 張って 歩きました。
sentence.reading: かれ は むね を はって あるきました。
sentence.romaji: kare wa mune o hatte arukimashita
sentence.english: He walked with his chest held high.

kunyomi: きわ.める, きわ.まる, きわ.み
fact: Japan is home to the Michelin-starred restaurant 寿司さいとう's menu item 極上 (gokujō), meaning 'the finest quality.' The kanji 極 is often used to describe something at the highest level.
memory: Imagine climbing to the very top of the tallest mountain. You've reached the highest possible point—the extreme limit. That is 極.

sentence.japanese: 彼 は 料理 の 技術 を 極めました。
sentence.reading: かれ は りょうり の ぎじゅつ を きわめました。
sentence.romaji: kare wa ryouri no gijutsu o kiwamemashita
sentence.english: He mastered the art of cooking.

fact: The famous Japanese expression 玉の輿 (tama no koshi) literally means 'jeweled carriage' and refers to marrying into a wealthy or noble family.
memory: Imagine holding a perfectly round, shiny jewel in your hand. Its smooth shape helps you remember 玉 means both 'ball' and 'jewel.'

sentence.japanese: 子ども が ボール を 玉 の ように 投げました。
sentence.reading: こども が ボール を たま の ように なげました。
sentence.romaji: kodomo ga booru o tama no you ni nagemashita
sentence.english: The child threw the ball like a round jewel.

fact: Japanese schools often calculate 平均点 (heikinten), meaning 'average score,' after examinations. Students pay close attention to how their score compares with the class average.
memory: Imagine sharing a cake equally among friends so everyone gets the same amount. Making everything equal helps you remember 均 means 'average' or 'even.'

sentence.japanese: 今回 の テスト の 平均 は 八十点でした。
sentence.reading: こんかい の テスト の へいきん は はちじゅってんでした。
sentence.romaji: konkai no tesuto no heikin wa hachijutten deshita
sentence.english: The average score on this test was 80 points.

fact: Japanese train stations, parks, and public buildings often display signs such as 禁煙 (No Smoking) and 立入禁止 (No Entry), making 禁 one of the most recognizable public notice kanji.
memory: Imagine a large red sign blocking your way. It clearly tells everyone 'Do not enter!' That warning helps you remember 禁 means 'to prohibit' or 'to forbid.'

sentence.japanese: ここで 写真 を 撮ること は 禁止です。
sentence.reading: ここで しゃしん を とること は きんしです。
sentence.romaji: koko de shashin o toru koto wa kinshi desu
sentence.english: Taking photos here is prohibited.

fact: Traditional Japanese homes often have a quiet corner called a 床の間 (tokonoma), where artwork or flowers are displayed. The word 隅 refers to corners or tucked-away places.
memory: Imagine dust collecting in the far corner of a room where nobody usually looks. That hidden corner helps you remember 隅 means 'corner.'

sentence.japanese: 猫 が 部屋 の 隅 に 座っています。
sentence.reading: ねこ が へや の すみ に すわっています。
sentence.romaji: neko ga heya no sumi ni suwatteimasu
sentence.english: The cat is sitting in the corner of the room.

fact: Japan has discovered many ancient kofun burial mounds through archaeological excavations. Archaeologists use 掘る (horu), meaning 'to dig,' to uncover these historical treasures.
memory: Imagine using a shovel with your hand (扌) to dig deep into the ground until you discover hidden treasure. That action helps you remember 掘 means 'to dig.'

sentence.japanese: 庭 に 穴 を 掘りました。
sentence.reading: にわ に あな を ほりました。
sentence.romaji: niwa ni ana o horimashita
sentence.english: I dug a hole in the garden.

fact: Japanese learners often hear the terms 音読み (on'yomi) and 訓読み (kun'yomi). The 訓 in 訓読み refers to the native Japanese reading of a kanji.
memory: Imagine a teacher giving careful instructions to students in class. Those lessons and guidance help you remember 訓 means 'instruction' or 'training.'

sentence.japanese: 消防士 は 毎日 訓練 を しています。
sentence.reading: しょうぼうし は まいにち くんれん を しています。
sentence.romaji: shouboushi wa mainichi kunren o shiteimasu
sentence.english: Firefighters train every day.

kunyomi: む.れる, む.れ, むら.がる
fact: Japan is famous for schools of sardines and flocks of cranes. The kanji 群 is used for groups of animals as well as crowds of people.
memory: Imagine a large flock of birds flying together across the sky. Since they move as one group, the image helps you remember 群 means 'group' or 'crowd.'

sentence.japanese: 羊 の 群れ が 草 を 食べています。
sentence.reading: ひつじ の むれ が くさ を たべています。
sentence.romaji: hitsuji no mure ga kusa o tabeteimasu
sentence.english: A flock of sheep is eating grass.

fact: The famous historical epic 『源平合戦』 tells the story of two rival military forces. Today, 軍 is commonly seen in words related to the military, sports teams, and organized groups.
memory: Imagine a line of soldiers marching together behind a military vehicle. Their organized formation helps you remember 軍 means 'army' or 'military.'

sentence.japanese: その 国 は 強い 軍 を 持っています。
sentence.reading: その くに は つよい ぐん を もっています。
sentence.romaji: sono kuni wa tsuyoi gun o motteimasu
sentence.english: That country has a strong military.

kunyomi: かたむ.く, かたむ.ける
fact: One of Japan's most photographed landmarks is the leaning pagoda at some historic temples. The verb 傾く (katamuku) is commonly used whenever something tilts or leans.
memory: Imagine a tall tree beginning to lean to one side during a strong wind. That tilted position helps you remember 傾 means 'to lean' or 'to incline.'

words.reading: みぎ に かたむく

sentence.japanese: 木 が 風 で 傾いています。
sentence.reading: き が かぜ で かたむいています。
sentence.romaji: ki ga kaze de katamuiteimasu
sentence.english: The tree is leaning because of the wind.

fact: Traditional Japanese martial arts use 型 (kata), fixed forms or patterns of movement passed down through generations. The same kanji is also used for product models and blood types.
memory: Imagine pouring clay into a mold to create identical objects. That mold gives everything the same shape or type, helping you remember 型.

sentence.japanese: これは 新型 の 車 です。
sentence.reading: これ は しんがた の くるま です。
sentence.romaji: kore wa shingata no kuruma desu
sentence.english: This is a new model of car.

fact: The Japanese language has a special system called 敬語 (keigo), respectful speech used when talking to customers, teachers, and elders. It is an important part of Japanese culture and etiquette.
memory: Imagine bowing politely to someone you greatly admire. That respectful gesture helps you remember 敬 means 'respect' or 'honor.'

sentence.japanese: 先生 を 心から 尊敬しています。
sentence.reading: せんせい を こころから そんけいしています。
sentence.romaji: sensei o kokoro kara sonkei shiteimasu
sentence.english: I sincerely respect my teacher.

fact: Traditional Japanese performing arts such as kabuki, nō, and rakugo are all considered 芸能 (geinō). The kanji 芸 is widely used for art, entertainment, and performance.
memory: Imagine an artist carefully practicing a performance until it becomes beautiful. That talent and artistic skill help you remember 芸 means 'art' or 'performance.'

sentence.japanese: 彼女 は 芸術 を 勉強しています。
sentence.reading: かのじょ は げいじゅつ を べんきょうしています。
sentence.romaji: kanojo wa geijutsu o benkyou shiteimasu
sentence.english: She is studying art.

fact: Japan has a rich theatrical tradition, including kabuki, nō, and bunraku. The word 劇場 (gekijō), meaning 'theater,' is commonly seen on signs near performance venues.
memory: Imagine actors performing dramatically on a brightly lit stage while the audience applauds. That exciting performance helps you remember 劇 means 'drama' or 'theater.'

sentence.japanese: 昨日 劇場 で 演劇 を 見ました。
sentence.reading: きのう げきじょう で えんげき を みました。
sentence.romaji: kinou gekijou de engeki o mimashita
sentence.english: Yesterday I watched a play at the theater.

fact: Japanese train stations use automatic ticket gates where passengers insert or tap their 券. The kanji appears on tickets for trains, movies, museums, and events.
memory: Imagine holding a train ticket tightly in your hand before passing through the station gate. That ticket reminds you that 券 means 'ticket' or 'voucher.'

sentence.japanese: 映画 の 入場券 を 買いました。
sentence.reading: えいが の にゅうじょうけん を かいました。
sentence.romaji: eiga no nyuujouken o kaimashita
sentence.english: I bought a movie ticket.

fact: Many Japanese offices offer 肩たたき (shoulder massage) machines or massage chairs because shoulder stiffness (肩こり) is a common problem among desk workers.
memory: Imagine carrying a heavy backpack all day until your shoulders become sore. That aching feeling helps you remember 肩 means 'shoulder.'

words.meaning: To put an arm around someone's shoulder

sentence.japanese: 長時間 の 仕事 で 肩 が 痛いです。
sentence.reading: ちょうじかん の しごと で かた が いたいです。
sentence.romaji: choujikan no shigoto de kata ga itai desu
sentence.english: My shoulders hurt from working for a long time.

fact: The Japanese expression 賢い選択 (kashikoi sentaku), meaning 'a wise choice,' is commonly used in advertisements encouraging customers to make smart decisions.
memory: Imagine an owl wearing glasses and reading books all night. The wise owl helps you remember that 賢 means 'wise' or 'clever.'

sentence.japanese: 彼女 は とても 賢い 学生です。
sentence.reading: かのじょ は とても かしこい がくせいです。
sentence.romaji: kanojo wa totemo kashikoi gakusei desu
sentence.english: She is a very clever student.

fact: Traditional Japanese houses have beautifully curved roof eaves called 軒 (noki), which help protect the walls from rain. The kanji is also used as a counter for houses and shops.
memory: Imagine standing under the roof's overhanging eaves while staying dry during a rainstorm. Those protective eaves remind you of 軒.

sentence.japanese: その 通り に 古い 家 が 十軒 あります。
sentence.reading: その とおり に ふるい いえ が じゅっけん あります。
sentence.romaji: sono toori ni furui ie ga jukken arimasu
sentence.english: There are ten old houses on that street.

fact: Japan's population has been decreasing for many years. News reports frequently use the word 人口減少 (jinkō genshō), meaning 'population decline,' making 減 a very common kanji in newspapers.
memory: Imagine water (氵) slowly draining from a bucket until there's less and less left. Watching it shrink helps you remember 減 means 'to decrease' or 'to reduce.'

sentence.japanese: ゴミ を 減らす 努力 を しています。
sentence.reading: ゴミ を へらす どりょく を しています。
sentence.romaji: gomi o herasu doryoku o shiteimasu
sentence.english: I am making an effort to reduce waste.

fact: 個 is the most common counter for small objects in Japanese. If you're unsure which counter to use, 個 often works for things like apples, eggs, or candies.
memory: Imagine counting apples one by one into a basket: one apple, two apples, three apples. Each individual item is counted with 個.

sentence.japanese: りんご を 三個 買いました。
sentence.reading: りんご を さんこ かいました。
sentence.romaji: ringo o sanko kaimashita
sentence.english: I bought three apples.

kunyomi: かた.い, かた.める, かた.まる
fact: The Japanese dessert プリン can become 固い (katai) if chilled too long. The kanji 固 is commonly used to describe anything hard, solid, or firmly fixed.
memory: Imagine a rock trapped inside a box that cannot move at all. Since it's completely firm and fixed, it reminds you that 固 means 'solid.'

sentence.japanese: この パン は 少し 固いです。
sentence.reading: この パン は すこし かたいです。
sentence.romaji: kono pan wa sukoshi katai desu
sentence.english: This bread is a little hard.

fact: Traditional Japanese kura (storehouses) were built with thick walls to protect rice, valuables, and documents from fire and humidity. Many historic 庫 buildings still survive today.
memory: Imagine a huge building filled with neatly stacked boxes from floor to ceiling. That giant storage building reminds you that 庫 means 'warehouse.'

sentence.japanese: 商品 は 倉庫 に 保管されています。
sentence.reading: しょうひん は そうこ に ほかんされています。
sentence.romaji: shouhin wa souko ni hokan sareteimasu
sentence.english: The products are stored in the warehouse.

fact: Japan's population is often measured by 世帯 (households), and older statistics used 戸 (ko) as the unit for counting households. You'll also see 戸 in words like 戸籍 (family register).
memory: Imagine opening the wooden front door of a traditional Japanese home. That single door represents one household, helping you remember 戸.

sentence.japanese: 戸 を 静かに 閉めてください。
sentence.reading: と を しずかに しめてください。
sentence.romaji: to o shizuka ni shimete kudasai
sentence.english: Please close the door quietly.

fact: In Japanese gardens, gardeners carefully remove 枯れ葉 (kareba), or withered leaves, to keep the landscape beautiful. The contrast between fresh green leaves and dried ones is an important part of seasonal appreciation.
memory: Imagine a tree (木) standing through a long drought until every leaf dries up and falls. That withered tree helps you remember 枯 means 'to wither' or 'to dry up.'

sentence.japanese: 庭 の 花 が 枯れてしまいました。
sentence.reading: にわ の はな が かれてしまいました。
sentence.romaji: niwa no hana ga karete shimaimashita
sentence.english: The flowers in the garden have withered.

fact: Japan's annual 新卒採用 (shinsotsu saiyō), or new graduate hiring season, is one of the largest recruitment periods in the country. Companies hire thousands of university graduates each year.
memory: Imagine a company shaking hands with a new employee on their first day. Hiring someone into the team helps you remember 雇 means 'to employ.'

sentence.japanese: 会社 は 新しい 社員 を 雇いました。
sentence.reading: かいしゃ は あたらしい しゃいん を やといました。
sentence.romaji: kaisha wa atarashii shain o yatoimashita
sentence.english: The company hired a new employee.

fact: Medicine packages in Japan often describe whether a drug is 効く (kiku), meaning 'it works,' or list 効果 (kōka), meaning 'its effectiveness.'
memory: Imagine taking medicine and feeling better within minutes. When something works exactly as intended, it reminds you of 効 meaning 'effective.'

sentence.japanese: この 薬 は よく 効きます。
sentence.reading: この くすり は よく ききます。
sentence.romaji: kono kusuri wa yoku kikimasu
sentence.english: This medicine works well.

fact: Japanese winter blankets are often advertised as 厚手 (atsude), meaning 'thick fabric.' The kanji 厚 is also used in words like 厚意, referring to someone's kindness or generosity.
memory: Imagine wrapping yourself in a thick blanket during winter. Its heavy warmth helps you remember 厚 means 'thick.'

sentence.japanese: この 本 は とても 厚いです。
sentence.reading: この ほん は とても あついです。
sentence.romaji: kono hon wa totemo atsui desu
sentence.english: This book is very thick.

fact: In Japan, eggs are often described as 半熟 (soft-boiled) or 固ゆで (hard-boiled). The kanji 硬 is also widely used in computing terms such as ハードディスク (hard disk) and 硬貨 (coins).
memory: Imagine trying to bite into a rock. It's so hard that your teeth can't leave a mark. That solid feeling helps you remember 硬 means 'hard' or 'stiff.'

sentence.japanese: この パン は 少し 硬いです。
sentence.reading: この パン は すこし かたいです。
sentence.romaji: kono pan wa sukoshi katai desu
sentence.english: This bread is a little hard.

fact: Every autumn, millions of people travel across Japan to enjoy 紅葉 (kōyō), the brilliant red and orange maple leaves. Autumn leaf viewing is one of Japan's most beloved seasonal traditions.
memory: Imagine maple leaves turning a beautiful deep crimson during autumn. That vivid red color helps you remember 紅 means 'deep red' or 'crimson.'

sentence.japanese: 秋 に 紅葉 を 見に 行きました。
sentence.reading: あき に こうよう を みに いきました。
sentence.romaji: aki ni kouyou o mi ni ikimashita
sentence.english: I went to see the autumn leaves.

fact: Rice cultivation has shaped Japanese culture for over 2,000 years. The verb 耕す (tagayasu), meaning 'to cultivate,' is closely connected with traditional rice farming.
memory: Imagine a farmer guiding a plow across a rice field to prepare the soil. That farming action helps you remember 耕 means 'to cultivate.'

sentence.japanese: 農家 が 畑 を 耕しています。
sentence.reading: のうか が はたけ を たがやしています。
sentence.romaji: nouka ga hatake o tagayashiteimasu
sentence.english: The farmer is cultivating the field.

fact: The word 肯定 (kōtei), meaning 'affirmation' or 'approval,' is widely used in education, psychology, and business. The opposite is 否定 (hitei), meaning 'denial.'
memory: Imagine nodding your head with a smile when someone asks for your approval. That positive response helps you remember 肯 means 'agree' or 'consent.'

sentence.japanese: 彼 は 私 の 意見 を 肯定しました。
sentence.reading: かれ は わたし の いけん を こうていしました。
sentence.romaji: kare wa watashi no iken o koutei shimashita
sentence.english: He agreed with my opinion.

fact: Japan's busiest airports display words like 航空券 (airline ticket) and 航空会社 (airline company). The kanji 航 is strongly associated with air and sea transportation.
memory: Imagine an airplane smoothly flying across the sky toward another country. That journey helps you remember 航 means 'to navigate' or 'to fly.'

sentence.japanese: 来月 日本 へ 航空機 で 行きます。
sentence.reading: らいげつ にほん へ こうくうき で いきます。
sentence.romaji: raigetsu nihon e koukuuki de ikimasu
sentence.english: Next month I will go to Japan by airplane.

kunyomi: あら.い, あ.れる, あ.らす
fact: Typhoons sometimes cause 荒れた天気 (areta tenki), meaning 'stormy weather.' Japanese weather forecasts frequently use this expression during the rainy and typhoon seasons.
memory: Imagine a peaceful sea suddenly becoming rough with giant crashing waves. That wild scene helps you remember 荒 means 'rough' or 'wild.'

sentence.japanese: 今日は 海 が 荒れています。
sentence.reading: きょう は うみ が あれています。
sentence.romaji: kyou wa umi ga areteimasu
sentence.english: The sea is rough today.

fact: Japanese universities often hold 公開講座 (kōkai kōza), or public lectures, where anyone can attend classes taught by professors. The kanji 講 frequently appears in education and business seminars.
memory: Imagine a teacher standing in front of a classroom, discussing an important topic while students take notes. That scene helps you remember 講 means 'lecture' or 'discuss.'

sentence.japanese: 大学 で 日本史 の 講義 を 受けました。
sentence.reading: だいがく で にほんし の こうぎ を うけました。
sentence.romaji: daigaku de nihonshi no kougi o ukemashita
sentence.english: I attended a lecture on Japanese history at university.

fact: Many Japanese families live in 郊外 (kōgai), the suburbs surrounding large cities like Tokyo and Osaka, then commute by train into the city center each day.
memory: Imagine leaving the busy city behind until you reach quiet neighborhoods with more space and trees. Those peaceful outskirts remind you that 郊 means 'suburbs.'

sentence.japanese: 彼 は 東京 の 郊外 に 住んでいます。
sentence.reading: かれ は とうきょう の こうがい に すんでいます。
sentence.romaji: kare wa toukyou no kougai ni sundeimasu
sentence.english: He lives in the suburbs of Tokyo.

fact: Although Japan has limited natural mineral resources, it historically operated famous mines such as the Iwami Ginzan Silver Mine, now a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The kanji 鉱 appears in words related to mining and minerals.
memory: Imagine miners digging deep underground until they discover shiny metal ore hidden inside the rock. That valuable ore reminds you that 鉱 means 'mineral' or 'ore.'

sentence.japanese: この 地域 は 鉱物 が 豊富です。
sentence.reading: この ちいき は こうぶつ が ほうふです。
sentence.romaji: kono chiiki wa koubutsu ga houfu desu
sentence.english: This region is rich in minerals.

fact: Traditional Japanese sitting styles such as seiza can place pressure on the lower back. The expression 腰が痛い (koshi ga itai), meaning 'my lower back hurts,' is very common in everyday conversation.
memory: Imagine lifting a heavy box and immediately grabbing your lower back in pain. That sore waist helps you remember 腰 means 'waist' or 'lower back.'

words.reading: こし を おろす

sentence.japanese: 重い 荷物 を 持って 腰 を 痛めました。
sentence.reading: おもい にもつ を もって こし を いためました。
sentence.romaji: omoi nimotsu o motte koshi o itamemashita
sentence.english: I hurt my lower back carrying heavy luggage.

fact: After cremation in Japan, family members use chopsticks together to carefully place the remaining bones into an urn during a ceremony called 骨上げ (kotsuage). This is a unique and important Japanese funeral custom.
memory: Imagine a skeleton standing upright, with every bone supporting the body. Those strong bones help you remember 骨 means 'bone.'

sentence.japanese: 彼 は 転んで 骨折 しました。
sentence.reading: かれ は ころんで こっせつ しました。
sentence.romaji: kare wa koronde kossetsu shimashita
sentence.english: He fell and broke a bone.

fact: One of Japan's most famous expressions is 根回し (nemawashi), literally 'going around the roots.' Originally a gardening term, it now means quietly building consensus before making an important decision in business or politics.
memory: Imagine a giant tree whose strong roots spread deep underground. The hidden roots support everything above, helping you remember 根 means 'root' or 'origin.'

sentence.japanese: 木 の 根 が 深く 広がっています。
sentence.reading: き の ね が ふかく ひろがっています。
sentence.romaji: ki no ne ga fukaku hirogatteimasu
sentence.english: The tree's roots spread deep underground.

kunyomi: ま.じる, ま.ざる, ま.ぜる, こ.む
fact: Japanese train stations often announce 電車が混んでいます (densha ga kondeimasu), meaning 'the train is crowded.' The kanji 混 commonly refers to mixing things together or being crowded.
memory: Imagine different colored paints being mixed together until you can't tell them apart. That blending helps you remember 混 means 'to mix.'

sentence.japanese: 朝 の 電車 は とても 混んでいます。
sentence.reading: あさ の でんしゃ は とても こんでいます。
sentence.romaji: asa no densha wa totemo kondeimasu
sentence.english: The train is very crowded in the morning.

fact: Police investigations in Japan are called 捜査 (sōsa), while companies conduct 調査 (chōsa) to research customer opinions and market trends. This kanji is common in news reports.
memory: Imagine a detective carefully examining every clue with a magnifying glass. His investigation helps you remember 査 means 'to investigate.'

sentence.japanese: 警察 が 事件 を 捜査しています。
sentence.reading: けいさつ が じけん を そうさしています。
sentence.romaji: keisatsu ga jiken o sousa shiteimasu
sentence.english: The police are investigating the incident.

fact: Japan's famous 鳥取砂丘 (Tottori Sand Dunes) are the country's largest sand dunes, stretching for about 16 kilometers along the Sea of Japan and attracting millions of visitors each year.
memory: Imagine scooping warm sand through your fingers at the beach. Those tiny grains help you remember 砂 means 'sand.'

sentence.japanese: 子ども たち は 砂 で 城 を 作りました。
sentence.reading: こどもたち は すな で しろ を つくりました。
sentence.romaji: kodomotachi wa suna de shiro o tsukurimashita
sentence.english: The children built a sandcastle.

fact: One of the first buttons many Japanese learners recognize on websites is 再生 (saisei), meaning 'play' or 'play again.' The kanji 再 is widely used for repeating, restarting, and trying again.
memory: Imagine pressing the replay button on a video after it finishes. Starting it once again helps you remember 再 means 'again' or 'repeat.'

sentence.japanese: もう 一度 再生 してください。
sentence.reading: もう いちど さいせい してください。
sentence.romaji: mou ichido saisei shite kudasai
sentence.english: Please play it again.

fact: Every spring, many Japanese families enjoy いちご狩り (strawberry picking) and tea leaf harvesting. The verb 採る (toru) is commonly used when gathering fruits, vegetables, tea leaves, or collecting samples.
memory: Imagine reaching out with your hand (扌) to pick a ripe apple from a tree. Using your hand to gather something helps you remember 採 means 'to pick' or 'to collect.'

sentence.japanese: 山 で きのこ を 採りました。
sentence.reading: やま で きのこ を とりました。
sentence.romaji: yama de kinoko o torimashita
sentence.english: I picked mushrooms in the mountains.

kunyomi: ほそ.い, こま.かい, ほそ.る, ほそ.める
fact: Japanese craftsmanship is famous for its 細かい (komakai) attention to detail. Whether making sushi, pottery, or electronics, careful precision is highly valued.
memory: Imagine drawing an extremely thin line with a sharp pencil. Its fine width helps you remember 細 means 'thin' or 'detailed.'

sentence.japanese: 細かい 説明 を 読みました。
sentence.reading: こまかい せつめい を よみました。
sentence.romaji: komakai setsumei o yomimashita
sentence.english: I read the detailed explanation.

fact: Japan has a long tradition of building wooden temples and shrines. The word 木材 (mokuzai), meaning 'timber,' is commonly used in construction and woodworking.
memory: Imagine stacks of wooden boards waiting to become a house. Those building materials help you remember 材 means 'material' or 'timber.'

sentence.japanese: この 料理 の 材料 は 新鮮です。
sentence.reading: この りょうり の ざいりょう は しんせんです。
sentence.romaji: kono ryouri no zairyou wa shinsen desu
sentence.english: The ingredients for this dish are fresh.

fact: Every spring, millions of people gather for 花見 (hanami), enjoying cherry blossoms in full bloom. The verb 咲く (saku) is used whenever flowers bloom.
memory: Imagine a cherry blossom slowly opening its petals until it fully blooms. That beautiful flower helps you remember 咲 means 'to bloom.'

words.reading: まんかい に さく
さくら が さく

sentence.japanese: 桜 が 美しく 咲いています。
sentence.reading: さくら が うつくしく さいています。
sentence.romaji: sakura ga utsukushiku saiteimasu
sentence.english: The cherry blossoms are blooming beautifully.

fact: 冊 is the counter used for books, notebooks, magazines, and manga. Japan publishes thousands of manga volumes every year, so learners encounter this counter very early.
memory: Imagine stacking several books neatly on a desk and counting them one by one: 一冊, 二冊, 三冊. That stack helps you remember 冊 is the counter for books.

sentence.japanese: 昨日 本 を 二冊 買いました。
sentence.reading: きのう ほん を にさつ かいました。
sentence.romaji: kinou hon o nisatsu kaimashita
sentence.english: I bought two books yesterday.

fact: Japan is one of the world's largest publishers of manga and books. Every new volume is 印刷 (insatsu), or printed, before reaching bookstores across the country.
memory: Imagine a printing press rolling fresh ink onto paper to create hundreds of books. That printing action helps you remember 刷 means 'to print.'

sentence.japanese: この 本 は 日本 で 印刷されました。
sentence.reading: この ほん は にほん で いんさつされました。
sentence.romaji: kono hon wa nihon de insatsu saremashita
sentence.english: This book was printed in Japan.

fact: Japanese paper money is called お札 (osatsu). The honorific prefix お is almost always used in everyday conversation when referring to banknotes.
memory: Imagine a price tag hanging from a product. That small tag reminds you of 札, which also means a paper banknote.

sentence.japanese: 財布 に 一万円札 が 入っています。
sentence.reading: さいふ に いちまんえんさつ が はいっています。
sentence.romaji: saifu ni ichiman'ensatsu ga haitteimasu
sentence.english: There is a 10,000-yen bill in my wallet.

fact: At conveyor-belt sushi restaurants in Japan, the price of your meal is often calculated by counting the number and color of empty 皿 (plates) stacked on your table.
memory: Imagine stacking clean dinner plates after washing the dishes. Those round plates help you remember 皿 means 'plate' or 'dish.'

sentence.japanese: 皿 を テーブル に 並べました。
sentence.reading: さら を テーブル に ならべました。
sentence.romaji: sara o teeburu ni narabemashita
sentence.english: I placed the plates on the table.

fact: Japanese elementary school students still learn mental arithmetic using そろばん (abacus). The kanji 算 appears in words like 計算 (calculation) and 算数 (elementary mathematics).
memory: Imagine sliding beads on an abacus while solving a math problem. That careful counting helps you remember 算 means 'to calculate.'

sentence.japanese: 電卓 を 使って 計算しました。
sentence.reading: でんたく を つかって けいさんしました。
sentence.romaji: dentaku o tsukatte keisan shimashita
sentence.english: I calculated it using a calculator.

fact: In Japanese business etiquette, 伺います (ukagaimasu) is humble language (謙譲語). It can mean 'I will visit,' 'I will ask,' or 'I will hear,' depending on the context.
memory: Imagine politely bowing before visiting someone's office and saying, 'May I come in?' That respectful visit helps you remember 伺 means 'to humbly visit' or 'to ask.'

words.reading: おはなし を うかがう
ごいけん を うかがう
のちほど うかがいます

sentence.japanese: 明日 お客様 の 会社 に 伺います。
sentence.reading: あした おきゃくさま の かいしゃ に うかがいます。
sentence.romaji: ashita okyakusama no kaisha ni ukagaimasu
sentence.english: I will visit the customer's company tomorrow.

kunyomi: さ.す, さ.さる, さ.し
fact: The word 刺身 (sashimi), literally meaning 'pierced body,' refers to sliced raw fish. Originally, a fish's fin was pierced into the slices to identify the type of fish being served.
memory: Imagine accidentally pricking your finger on a rose thorn. That sharp piercing feeling helps you remember 刺 means 'to stab' or 'to prick.'

sentence.japanese: 指 に とげ が 刺さりました。
sentence.reading: ゆび に とげ が ささりました。
sentence.romaji: yubi ni toge ga sasarimashita
sentence.english: A thorn got stuck in my finger.

fact: Japanese students study 日本史 (Nihonshi), the history of Japan, throughout school. Historic events such as the samurai era and the Meiji Restoration are central topics.
memory: Imagine reading a thick history book filled with stories from long ago. Those records of the past help you remember 史 means 'history.'

sentence.japanese: 私は 日本史 に 興味 が あります。
sentence.reading: わたし は にほんし に きょうみ が あります。
sentence.romaji: watashi wa nihonshi ni kyoumi ga arimasu
sentence.english: I am interested in Japanese history.

fact: Cherry blossom branches are carefully arranged in the Japanese art of ikebana. Choosing the right 枝 (eda) is an important part of creating a balanced floral arrangement.
memory: Imagine a tree stretching many branches toward the sky. Each branch growing from the trunk helps you remember 枝 means 'branch.'

sentence.japanese: 鳥 が 木 の 枝 に 止まっています。
sentence.reading: とり が き の えだ に とまっています。
sentence.romaji: tori ga ki no eda ni tomatteimasu
sentence.english: A bird is perched on a tree branch.

fact: Silk production played an important role in Japan's modernization during the Meiji era. Fine silk thread (糸) became one of the country's major exports.
memory: Imagine holding a long piece of thread between your fingers while sewing. That thin string helps you remember 糸 means 'thread.'

sentence.japanese: 母 は 糸 と 針 で 服 を 直しました。
sentence.reading: はは は いと と はり で ふく を なおしました。
sentence.romaji: haha wa ito to hari de fuku o naoshimashita
sentence.english: My mother repaired the clothes with thread and a needle.

fact: The famous marbled beef known as 和牛 (wagyū) is prized for its rich 脂 (abura), or fat, which melts at a low temperature and gives the meat its buttery texture.
memory: Imagine bacon sizzling in a frying pan as shiny grease drips from it. That oily fat helps you remember 脂 means 'fat' or 'grease.'

sentence.japanese: この 肉 は 脂 が 多いです。
sentence.reading: この にく は あぶら が おおいです。
sentence.romaji: kono niku wa abura ga ooi desu
sentence.english: This meat has a lot of fat.

fact: Japanese songs often distinguish between 曲 (melody) and 歌詞 (kashi), the song's lyrics. Karaoke lovers frequently read 歌詞 on the screen while singing.
memory: Imagine reading the words of your favorite song on a karaoke screen. Those written lyrics help you remember 詞 means 'words' or 'lyrics.'

sentence.japanese: この 歌 の 歌詞 を 覚えました。
sentence.reading: この うた の かし を おぼえました。
sentence.romaji: kono uta no kashi o oboemashita
sentence.english: I memorized the lyrics of this song.

fact: Japan publishes thousands of manga magazines every year. The kanji 誌 appears in words like 雑誌 (magazine) and 学会誌 (academic journal).
memory: Imagine flipping through the colorful pages of a magazine filled with articles and photos. That publication helps you remember 誌 means 'magazine.'

sentence.japanese: 駅 で 新しい 雑誌 を 買いました。
sentence.reading: えき で あたらしい ざっし を かいました。
sentence.romaji: eki de atarashii zasshi o kaimashita
sentence.english: I bought a new magazine at the station.

fact: Japan celebrates Children's Day every May 5. Many official words related to children use 児, such as 小児科 (pediatrics) and 幼児 (young child).
memory: Imagine a happy child running through a park while flying a colorful carp streamer. That cheerful child helps you remember 児 means 'child.'

sentence.japanese: その 病院 は 小児科 が 有名です。
sentence.reading: その びょういん は しょうにか が ゆうめいです。
sentence.romaji: sono byouin wa shounika ga yuumei desu
sentence.english: That hospital is well known for its pediatrics department.

fact: Japan's rainy season, 梅雨 (tsuyu), is known for its high humidity. The word 湿度 (shitsudo), meaning 'humidity,' appears frequently in weather forecasts during early summer.
memory: Imagine your clothes becoming damp on a hot, humid day. That sticky feeling helps you remember 湿 means 'moist' or 'humid.'

sentence.japanese: 今日は 湿度 が 高いです。
sentence.reading: きょう は しつど が たかいです。
sentence.romaji: kyou wa shitsudo ga takai desu
sentence.english: The humidity is high today.

fact: Japan has strict garbage sorting rules. Residents must separate recyclables, burnable trash, and non-burnable trash before 捨てる (suteru), or throwing them away.
memory: Imagine dropping an old newspaper into a recycling bin. Throwing something away helps you remember 捨 means 'to discard.'

sentence.japanese: 古い 雑誌 を 捨てました。
sentence.reading: ふるい ざっし を すてました。
sentence.romaji: furui zasshi o sutemashita
sentence.english: I threw away the old magazines.

fact: The word 一週間 (isshūkan), meaning 'one week,' literally means 'one cycle around.' The kanji 周 is often used to describe something that goes around or completes a full circuit.
memory: Imagine walking all the way around a park until you return to your starting point. Completing the circle helps you remember 周 means 'around.'

sentence.japanese: 公園 を 一周 しました。
sentence.reading: こうえん を いっしゅう しました。
sentence.romaji: kouen o isshuu shimashita
sentence.english: I walked one lap around the park.

fact: Japan is divided into regions rather than states, but 州 appears in names such as 九州 (Kyūshū) and in foreign place names like 欧州 (Europe) and 米州 (the Americas).
memory: Imagine looking at a world map divided into different states and regions. Each large region reminds you that 州 means 'state' or 'province.'

sentence.japanese: 九州 は 温泉 が 多い 地域 です。
sentence.reading: きゅうしゅう は おんせん が おおい ちいき です。
sentence.romaji: kyuushuu wa onsen ga ooi chiiki desu
sentence.english: Kyushu is a region with many hot springs.

fact: Japan is famous for its low crime rate, and it is common for lost wallets or phones to be 拾われる (hirowareru) and turned in to the nearest police box (交番).
memory: Imagine seeing a coin on the sidewalk and bending down to pick it up with your hand. That action helps you remember 拾 means 'to pick up.'

words.reading: ごみ を ひろう

sentence.japanese: 道 で 財布 を 拾いました。
sentence.reading: みち で さいふ を ひろいました。
sentence.romaji: michi de saifu o hiroimashita
sentence.english: I found a wallet on the road.

fact: Traditional wooden boats called 屋形舟 (yakatabune) still cruise Tokyo Bay and rivers, offering meals and beautiful nighttime views of the city skyline.
memory: Imagine a small wooden boat floating peacefully on a calm river. That simple boat helps you remember 舟 means 'boat.'

sentence.japanese: 小さな 舟 が 川 を 渡っています。
sentence.reading: ちいさな ふね が かわ を わたっています。
sentence.romaji: chiisana fune ga kawa o watatteimasu
sentence.english: A small boat is crossing the river.

kunyomi: やわ.らか, やわ.らかい
fact: The martial art 柔道 (jūdō) literally means 'the gentle way.' Instead of relying on strength alone, it emphasizes technique, balance, and using an opponent's force against them.
memory: Imagine squeezing a soft pillow that easily changes shape in your hands. Its gentle softness helps you remember 柔 means 'soft' or 'gentle.'

sentence.japanese: この パン は とても 柔らかいです。
sentence.reading: この パン は とても やわらかいです。
sentence.romaji: kono pan wa totemo yawarakai desu
sentence.english: This bread is very soft.

fact: The word 述べる (noberu) appears frequently in Japanese news when politicians or company presidents express their opinions. Headlines often use phrases like 『首相が考えを述べた』 ('The Prime Minister stated his views').
memory: Imagine standing at a podium carefully explaining your opinion to an audience. Speaking clearly helps you remember 述 means 'to state' or 'to describe.'

words.reading: いけん を のべる

sentence.japanese: 彼 は 自分 の 意見 を 述べました。
sentence.reading: かれ は じぶん の いけん を のべました。
sentence.romaji: kare wa jibun no iken o nobemashita
sentence.english: He expressed his opinion.

fact: Japan Industrial Standards (JIS) help ensure the quality of products ranging from screws to electronics. The kanji 準 appears in words related to standards and preparation.
memory: Imagine carefully lining up runners at the starting line before a race begins. Getting everything ready helps you remember 準 means 'prepare' or 'standard.'

sentence.japanese: 旅行 の 準備 が できました。
sentence.reading: りょこう の じゅんび が できました。
sentence.romaji: ryokou no junbi ga dekimashita
sentence.english: The preparations for the trip are complete.

fact: Jewelry in Japan is often labeled 純金 (junkin), meaning 'pure gold,' or 純銀 (jungin), meaning 'pure silver.' The kanji 純 is widely used to indicate something genuine or unmixed.
memory: Imagine crystal-clear water without even a single speck of dirt. Its perfect purity helps you remember 純 means 'pure.'

sentence.japanese: これは 純金 の 指輪 です。
sentence.reading: これ は じゅんきん の ゆびわ です。
sentence.romaji: kore wa junkin no yubiwa desu
sentence.english: This is a pure gold ring.

fact: When waiting for trains or elevators in Japan, people usually line up in 順番 (junban), meaning 'proper order.' Forming orderly queues is an important part of everyday etiquette.
memory: Imagine people patiently standing in a neat line, each waiting for their turn. That orderly sequence helps you remember 順 means 'order.'

sentence.japanese: 順番 を 守って ください。
sentence.reading: じゅんばん を まもって ください。
sentence.romaji: junban o mamotte kudasai
sentence.english: Please wait your turn.

fact: Books in Japan often display the author's name followed by 著 (cho), meaning 'written by.' For example: 『村上春樹 著』 means 'Written by Haruki Murakami.'
memory: Imagine an author proudly holding a newly published book with their name on the cover. That published work helps you remember 著 means 'to write' or 'author.'

sentence.japanese: この 本 の 著者 は 有名です。
sentence.reading: この ほん の ちょしゃ は ゆうめいです。
sentence.romaji: kono hon no chosha wa yuumei desu
sentence.english: The author of this book is famous.

fact: Government announcements and official documents frequently begin with 諸外国 (shogaikoku, 'various foreign countries') or 諸問題 (shomondai, 'various issues'). The kanji 諸 often gives writing a formal tone.
memory: Imagine many different flags gathered together at an international meeting. Seeing many countries in one place helps you remember 諸 means 'various' or 'many.'

sentence.japanese: 諸事情 に より 会議 は 中止 です。
sentence.reading: しょじじょう に より かいぎ は ちゅうし です。
sentence.romaji: shojijou ni yori kaigi wa chuushi desu
sentence.english: Due to various circumstances, the meeting has been canceled.

fact: In very polite Japanese, the honorific expression 召し上がる (meshiagaru) means 'to eat' or 'to drink.' It is commonly used when speaking respectfully to customers or guests.
memory: Imagine a king ringing a bell to summon someone into the throne room. That royal invitation helps you remember 召 means 'to summon.'

sentence.japanese: どうぞ 温かいうち に お召し上がりください。
sentence.reading: どうぞ あたたかい うち に おめしあがりください。
sentence.romaji: douzo atatakai uchi ni omeshiagari kudasai
sentence.english: Please enjoy it while it's still warm.

fact: The highest piece in Japanese chess (将棋, shōgi) is the 王将 (ōshō), or king general. The kanji 将 is also used in words related to the future, such as 将来 (future).
memory: Imagine a military commander leading an army while planning for the future. That leader helps you remember 将 means 'commander' or 'future.'

sentence.japanese: 将来 日本 で 働きたいです。
sentence.reading: しょうらい にほん で はたらきたいです。
sentence.romaji: shourai nihon de hatarakitai desu
sentence.english: I want to work in Japan in the future.

fact: Traditional Japanese homes often have tatami floors. The word 床の間 (tokonoma) refers to a decorative alcove where scrolls and flower arrangements are displayed.
memory: Imagine walking barefoot across a smooth wooden floor before laying out a futon for bed. That floor helps you remember 床 means 'floor' or 'bed.'

sentence.japanese: 子ども が 床 に 座っています。
sentence.reading: こども が ゆか に すわっています。
sentence.romaji: kodomo ga yuka ni suwatteimasu
sentence.english: The child is sitting on the floor.

fact: When customer service staff in Japan answer the phone, they often say 承知しました (shōchi shimashita), meaning 'Certainly' or 'Understood.' This expression is considered very polite.
memory: Imagine respectfully bowing after receiving important instructions from your manager. Accepting the request helps you remember 承 means 'to accept' or 'to receive.'

sentence.japanese: はい、承知しました。
sentence.reading: はい、しょうちしました。
sentence.romaji: hai, shouchi shimashita
sentence.english: Yes, understood.

fact: Japan's famous Mount Fuji is a popular destination for 登山 (mountain climbing). When climbers reach the summit, they have completed the ascent, making 昇 a common kanji in words related to rising or promotion.
memory: Imagine climbing a long staircase until you reach the very top. Moving upward step by step helps you remember 昇 means 'to rise' or 'to ascend.'

sentence.japanese: 彼 は 部長 に 昇進しました。
sentence.reading: かれ は ぶちょう に しょうしんしました。
sentence.romaji: kare wa buchou ni shoushin shimashita
sentence.english: He was promoted to department manager.

kunyomi: や.く, や.ける, や.き
fact: Many famous Japanese dishes include this kanji, such as 焼き鳥 (yakitori), 焼き魚 (grilled fish), and お好み焼き (okonomiyaki). The word is closely connected with Japanese food culture.
memory: Imagine delicious food sizzling over glowing charcoal until it becomes perfectly grilled. That cooking scene helps you remember 焼 means 'to grill' or 'to burn.'

sentence.japanese: 夕食 に 魚 を 焼きました。
sentence.reading: ゆうしょく に さかな を やきました。
sentence.romaji: yuushoku ni sakana o yakimashita
sentence.english: I grilled fish for dinner.

kunyomi: て.る, て.らす, て.れる
fact: The sun goddess Amaterasu (天照大神) is one of the most important deities in Japanese mythology. Her name literally means 'Great Deity Who Shines Upon Heaven,' using the kanji 照.
memory: Imagine a bright flashlight shining into a dark room until everything becomes visible. That beam of light helps you remember 照 means 'to illuminate.'

words.word: 写真を撮る時に照らす
words.reading: しゃしん を とる とき に てらす
words.romaji: shashin o toru toki ni terasu

sentence.japanese: 太陽 が 海 を 明るく 照らしています。
sentence.reading: たいよう が うみ を あかるく てらしています。
sentence.romaji: taiyou ga umi o akaruku terashiteimasu
sentence.english: The sun is brightly shining on the sea.

kunyomi: かえり.みる, はぶ.く
fact: Most Japanese government departments end with the kanji 省, such as 外務省 (Ministry of Foreign Affairs) and 文部科学省 (Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology).
memory: Imagine a government ministry where officials carefully reflect on important decisions before acting. That thoughtful place helps you remember 省 means 'ministry' or 'to reflect.'

sentence.japanese: 失敗 を 反省 しました。
sentence.reading: しっぱい を はんせい しました。
sentence.romaji: shippai o hansei shimashita
sentence.english: I reflected on my mistake.

fact: Books, laws, and official documents in Japan are divided into chapters called 章 (shō). You'll also see this kanji in 文章 (writing) and 憲章 (charter).
memory: Imagine finishing one chapter of a book before turning the page to begin the next. That organized section helps you remember 章 means 'chapter.'

sentence.japanese: 今日は 第一章 を 読みました。
sentence.reading: きょう は だいいっしょう を よみました。
sentence.romaji: kyou wa daiisshou o yomimashita
sentence.english: Today I read Chapter One.

fact: The word 紹介 (shōkai), meaning 'introduction,' is one of the most common business and everyday expressions in Japanese. You'll often hear 自己紹介 (jikoshōkai) when people introduce themselves.
memory: Imagine two friends shaking hands after someone introduces them. That friendly introduction helps you remember 紹 means 'to introduce.'
