sentence.japanese: 先生 が 新しい 学生 を 紹介しました。
sentence.reading: せんせい が あたらしい がくせい を しょうかいしました。
sentence.romaji: sensei ga atarashii gakusei o shoukai shimashita
sentence.english: The teacher introduced the new student.

fact: Although elephants are not native to Japan, the kanji 象 appears in many abstract words such as 印象 (impression), 現象 (phenomenon), and 対象 (target).
memory: Imagine a huge elephant leaving a strong impression wherever it walks. That unforgettable image helps you remember 象 means 'elephant' and also appears in words related to impressions and phenomena.

sentence.japanese: 彼 は 良い 印象 を 与えました。
sentence.reading: かれ は よい いんしょう を あたえました。
sentence.romaji: kare wa yoi inshou o ataemashita
sentence.english: He made a good impression.

fact: Japan presents many prestigious awards each year, including the 芥川賞 (Akutagawa Prize), one of the country's most respected literary awards.
memory: Imagine standing on a stage while receiving a shiny trophy and applause. Winning that prize helps you remember 賞 means 'award.'

sentence.japanese: 彼女 は 最優秀賞 を 受賞しました。
sentence.reading: かのじょ は さいゆうしゅうしょう を じゅしょうしました。
sentence.romaji: kanojo wa saiyuushuushou o jushou shimashita
sentence.english: She won the top prize.

fact: Japan is home to many beautiful castles, including Himeji Castle, a UNESCO World Heritage Site often called the 'White Heron Castle' because of its elegant white exterior.
memory: Imagine a magnificent castle standing on a hill with high stone walls protecting it. That fortress helps you remember 城 means 'castle.'

sentence.japanese: 週末 に お城 を 見学しました。
sentence.reading: しゅうまつ に おしろ を けんがくしました。
sentence.romaji: shuumatsu ni oshiro o kengaku shimashita
sentence.english: I visited a castle over the weekend.

kunyomi: む.す, む.れる, む.らす
fact: Steaming is an important cooking method in Japan. Popular dishes such as 茶碗蒸し (chawanmushi), a savory steamed egg custard, use this kanji.
memory: Imagine hot steam rising from a bamboo steamer filled with delicious dumplings. That rising steam helps you remember 蒸 means 'to steam.'

sentence.japanese: 野菜 を 蒸して 食べました。
sentence.reading: やさい を むして たべました。
sentence.romaji: yasai o mushite tabemashita
sentence.english: I ate steamed vegetables.

fact: Every spring, volunteers across Japan participate in tree-planting events to restore forests after natural disasters. The verb 植える (ueru) means 'to plant' and is commonly used for trees, flowers, and crops.
memory: Imagine carefully placing a young tree into the soil and covering its roots with earth. That planting action helps you remember 植 means 'to plant.'

sentence.japanese: 庭 に 花 を 植えました。
sentence.reading: にわ に はな を うえました。
sentence.romaji: niwa ni hana o uemashita
sentence.english: I planted flowers in the garden.

fact: Many Japanese museums have signs reading 『触らないでください』 ('Please do not touch'). The kanji 触 is frequently used in everyday notices and warnings.
memory: Imagine reaching out with your finger to touch a delicate piece of art in a museum. That gentle contact helps you remember 触 means 'to touch.'

sentence.japanese: 展示品 に 触らないでください。
sentence.reading: てんじひん に さわらないでください。
sentence.romaji: tenjihin ni sawaranaide kudasai
sentence.english: Please do not touch the exhibits.

kunyomi: の.びる, の.ばす, の.べる
fact: Many Japanese offices begin the day with simple stretching exercises called ラジオ体操 (Radio Taisō). Stretching your body is described with the kanji 伸.
memory: Imagine waking up in the morning and stretching both arms high above your head. That movement helps you remember 伸 means 'to stretch.'

words.reading: せいちょう が のびる

sentence.japanese: 運動 の 前 に 体 を 伸ばします。
sentence.reading: うんどう の まえ に からだ を のばします。
sentence.romaji: undou no mae ni karada o nobashimasu
sentence.english: I stretch my body before exercising.

fact: Many Japanese cabinet ministers have titles ending in 大臣 (daijin), such as 外務大臣 (Minister for Foreign Affairs). The kanji 臣 has been associated with government officials for centuries.
memory: Imagine a trusted royal minister standing beside the king to offer advice. That loyal official helps you remember 臣 means 'minister.'

sentence.japanese: 新しい 大臣 が 任命されました。
sentence.reading: あたらしい だいじん が にんめいされました。
sentence.romaji: atarashii daijin ga ninmei saremashita
sentence.english: A new cabinet minister was appointed.

fact: Japanese curry restaurants often let customers choose the level of 辛さ (karasa), or spiciness. The same kanji is also used in 辛い (tsurai) to describe emotional hardship or difficult situations.
memory: Imagine eating an extremely spicy chili pepper that makes your eyes water. That burning sensation helps you remember 辛 means 'spicy' and also 'painful.'

sentence.japanese: この カレー は とても 辛いです。
sentence.reading: この カレー は とても からいです。
sentence.romaji: kono karee wa totemo karai desu
sentence.english: This curry is very spicy.

fact: Traditional Japanese embroidery (刺繍) and kimono making rely on fine sewing needles. The kanji 針 is also used for the hands of a clock, such as 長針 (minute hand) and 短針 (hour hand).
memory: Imagine threading a tiny needle before carefully sewing a piece of cloth. That sharp sewing needle helps you remember 針 means 'needle.'

sentence.japanese: 時計 の 長針 を 見てください。
sentence.reading: とけい の ちょうしん を みてください。
sentence.romaji: tokei no choushin o mite kudasai
sentence.english: Please look at the minute hand of the clock.

fact: Because Japan experiences frequent earthquakes, the word 地震 (jishin), meaning 'earthquake,' is one of the first disaster-related words many Japanese learners encounter.
memory: Imagine the ground suddenly shaking beneath your feet during an earthquake. That trembling earth helps you remember 震 means 'to shake.'

sentence.japanese: 昨夜 大きな 地震 が ありました。
sentence.reading: さくや おおきな じしん が ありました。
sentence.romaji: sakuya ookina jishin ga arimashita
sentence.english: There was a big earthquake last night.

fact: Sports commentators often use 勢い (ikioi), meaning 'momentum,' when describing a team that is rapidly gaining confidence and winning games.
memory: Imagine a fast-moving river rushing downhill with unstoppable force. That powerful momentum helps you remember 勢 means 'force' or 'energy.'

sentence.japanese: 彼 は 勢い よく 走りました。
sentence.reading: かれ は いきおい よく はしりました。
sentence.romaji: kare wa ikioi yoku hashirimashita
sentence.english: He ran with great energy.

fact: In Japanese, the family name (姓) is traditionally written before the given name. For example, 山田太郎 is read as 'Yamada Tarō,' with 山田 being the surname.
memory: Imagine introducing yourself by saying your family name first, just as people commonly do in Japan. That tradition helps you remember 姓 means 'surname.'

sentence.japanese: 日本 では 姓 を 先 に 書きます。
sentence.reading: にほん では せい を さき に かきます。
sentence.romaji: nihon dewa sei o saki ni kakimasu
sentence.english: In Japan, the family name is written first.

fact: Every July, Japan celebrates the Tanabata Festival, where people write wishes on colorful strips of paper inspired by the meeting of the stars Vega and Altair across the Milky Way.
memory: Imagine looking up at a sky filled with bright, sparkling stars on a clear summer night. Those shining lights help you remember 星 means 'star.'

sentence.japanese: 今夜 は 星 が よく 見えます。
sentence.reading: こんや は ほし が よく みえます。
sentence.romaji: konya wa hoshi ga yoku miemasu
sentence.english: The stars are clearly visible tonight.

kunyomi: きよ.い, きよ.める, きよ.まる
fact: One of Japan's most famous temples, Kiyomizu-dera (清水寺), literally means 'Pure Water Temple.' Visitors drink from its sacred waterfall, believing it brings health, success, or wisdom.
memory: Imagine crystal-clear water flowing down a mountain stream without a single speck of dirt. That pure water helps you remember 清 means 'clean' or 'pure.'

sentence.japanese: 部屋 を 清潔 に 保ちましょう。
sentence.reading: へや を せいけつ に たもちましょう。
sentence.romaji: heya o seiketsu ni tamochimashou
sentence.english: Let's keep the room clean.

fact: When shopping in Japan, you'll often see 税込 (including tax) and 税別 (excluding tax). Since the consumption tax is added to many purchases, this kanji appears in stores everywhere.
memory: Imagine paying for an item at the checkout and noticing extra money added for tax. That receipt reminds you that 税 means 'tax.'

sentence.japanese: この 値段 は 税込 です。
sentence.reading: この ねだん は ぜいこみ です。
sentence.romaji: kono nedan wa zeikomi desu
sentence.english: This price includes tax.

meaning: Counter for Ships, One of a Pair
fact: 隻 is the counter used for large boats and ships in Japanese. It is also used in expressions like 片手だけ (one hand), referring to one side of a pair in formal language.
memory: Imagine counting giant ships in a harbor one by one. Each enormous vessel reminds you that 隻 is the counter for ships.

sentence.japanese: 港 に 船 が 一隻 停まっています。
sentence.reading: みなと に ふね が いっせき とまっています。
sentence.romaji: minato ni fune ga isseki tomatteimasu
sentence.english: One ship is docked at the harbor.

fact: Japan maintains a family registration system called 戸籍 (koseki), which officially records births, marriages, and family relationships. It is a unique part of Japan's legal system.
memory: Imagine your name being carefully written into an official government register. That record helps you remember 籍 means 'register' or 'membership.'

sentence.japanese: 彼 は 大学 に 在籍しています。
sentence.reading: かれ は だいがく に ざいせきしています。
sentence.romaji: kare wa daigaku ni zaiseki shiteimasu
sentence.english: He is enrolled at the university.

fact: Japanese schools often use 成績 (seiseki) to refer to students' grades. Companies also evaluate employees based on 業績 (gyōseki), meaning business performance or achievements.
memory: Imagine proudly receiving a report card full of excellent grades. Those achievements help you remember 績 means 'results' or 'achievement.'

sentence.japanese: 今学期 は 成績 が 良かったです。
sentence.reading: こんがっき は せいせき が よかったです。
sentence.romaji: kongakki wa seiseki ga yokatta desu
sentence.english: My grades were good this semester.

fact: Japan preserves many historic castle ruins and archaeological sites called 遺跡 (iseki). Famous places like the Yoshinogari Ruins allow visitors to walk through ancient Japanese history.
memory: Imagine seeing footprints left behind in fresh snow. Those traces remind you that 跡 means 'tracks' or 'trace.'

sentence.japanese: 雪 に 動物 の 足跡 が ありました。
sentence.reading: ゆき に どうぶつ の あしあと が ありました。
sentence.romaji: yuki ni doubutsu no ashiato ga arimashita
sentence.english: There were animal footprints in the snow.

fact: In Japanese business emails, people often write 『ご連絡ください』 when asking someone to get in touch. Words like 接客 (customer service) and 接続 (connection) are also very common.
memory: Imagine shaking hands with someone for the first time. That physical contact helps you remember 接 means 'to connect' or 'to contact.'

sentence.japanese: 明日 会社 の 面接 が あります。
sentence.reading: あした かいしゃ の めんせつ が あります。
sentence.romaji: ashita kaisha no mensetsu ga arimasu
sentence.english: I have a job interview tomorrow.

fact: Many Japanese universities were established more than a century ago. The kanji 設 appears in words like 設立 (establishment) and 建設 (construction).
memory: Imagine workers building a brand-new school from the ground up. Creating something new helps you remember 設 means 'to establish.'

sentence.japanese: 新しい 工場 を 建設しています。
sentence.reading: あたらしい こうじょう を けんせつしています。
sentence.romaji: atarashii koujou o kensetsu shiteimasu
sentence.english: They are building a new factory.

fact: Fortune telling (占い) remains popular in Japan. Many shrines and shopping districts have fortune-telling booths where visitors seek advice about love, careers, and health.
memory: Imagine a fortune teller reading tarot cards to predict your future. That scene helps you remember 占 means 'to tell fortunes.'

sentence.japanese: 友達 と 占い を 楽しみました。
sentence.reading: ともだち と うらない を たのしみました。
sentence.romaji: tomodachi to uranai o tanoshimimashita
sentence.english: I enjoyed fortune telling with my friend.

fact: Many Japanese colleges are called 専門学校 (senmon gakkō), meaning vocational or specialized schools. Students attend them to gain practical skills for specific careers.
memory: Imagine a student studying only one subject every day until becoming an expert. Focusing on one specialty helps you remember 専 means 'specialize.'

sentence.japanese: 大学 で 経済学 を 専攻しています。
sentence.reading: だいがく で けいざいがく を せんこうしています。
sentence.romaji: daigaku de keizaigaku o senkou shiteimasu
sentence.english: I am majoring in economics at university.

fact: Japan is famous for its thousands of natural hot springs (温泉, onsen). The kanji 泉 originally means a natural spring, the source from which fresh water flows.
memory: Imagine crystal-clear water bubbling up naturally from the ground. That fresh spring helps you remember 泉 means 'spring' or 'fountain.'

sentence.japanese: 温泉 で ゆっくり 過ごしました。
sentence.reading: おんせん で ゆっくり すごしました。
sentence.romaji: onsen de yukkuri sugoshimashita
sentence.english: I relaxed at a hot spring.

fact: The famous Asakusa district in Tokyo is written 浅草 ('shallow grass'). Although the place name has a historical origin, learners often recognize the first kanji because of its meaning 'shallow.'
memory: Imagine stepping into a river where the water only reaches your ankles. That shallow water helps you remember 浅 means 'shallow.'

sentence.japanese: この 川 は 浅いです。
sentence.reading: この かわ は あさいです。
sentence.romaji: kono kawa wa asai desu
sentence.english: This river is shallow.

fact: Japanese railway lines are called ～線 (sen), such as 山手線 (Yamanote Line) and 東海道新幹線 (Tokaido Shinkansen). This kanji appears everywhere on train maps.
memory: Imagine drawing one long straight line across a sheet of paper. That continuous line helps you remember 線 means 'line.'

sentence.japanese: 新幹線 で 大阪 へ 行きました。
sentence.reading: しんかんせん で おおさか へ いきました。
sentence.romaji: shinkansen de Oosaka e ikimashita
sentence.english: I went to Osaka by bullet train.

fact: The kanji 双 appears in words like 双子 (futago, twins) and 双方 (both parties). It is often used when referring to two matching or corresponding things.
memory: Imagine a pair of identical twins standing side by side. Seeing two matching people helps you remember 双 means 'pair' or 'double.'

sentence.japanese: 双方 が 合意しました。
sentence.reading: そうほう が ごういしました。
sentence.romaji: souhou ga goui shimashita
sentence.english: Both parties reached an agreement.

fact: Japanese marketers often divide customers into 年齢層 (age groups) or 消費者層 (consumer segments). The kanji 層 is commonly used for layers, social groups, and geological strata.
memory: Imagine a colorful cake with several layers stacked neatly on top of one another. Those layers help you remember 層 means 'layer.'

sentence.japanese: この 商品 は 若い 年齢層 に 人気 です。
sentence.reading: この しょうひん は わかい ねんれいそう に にんき です。
sentence.romaji: kono shouhin wa wakai nenreisou ni ninki desu
sentence.english: This product is popular among younger age groups.

fact: Japanese police investigations frequently use the word 捜査 (sōsa), meaning 'investigation.' News reports often mention police searching for suspects or missing persons using this kanji.
memory: Imagine a detective carefully searching every room for clues with a flashlight. That investigation helps you remember 捜 means 'to search.'

sentence.japanese: 警察 が 行方不明 の 人 を 捜しています。
sentence.reading: けいさつ が ゆくえふめい の ひと を さがしています。
sentence.romaji: keisatsu ga yukuefumei no hito o sagashiteimasu
sentence.english: The police are searching for the missing person.

fact: Many Japanese schools have students clean their own classrooms every day. This tradition, called 掃除 (sōji), teaches responsibility and respect for shared spaces.
memory: Imagine sweeping the floor with a broom until every bit of dust disappears. That cleaning action helps you remember 掃 means 'to sweep.'

sentence.japanese: 毎朝 教室 を 掃除します。
sentence.reading: まいあさ きょうしつ を そうじします。
sentence.romaji: maiasa kyoushitsu o souji shimasu
sentence.english: We clean the classroom every morning.

fact: During Japan's dry winter months, weather reports often mention 乾燥 (kansō), meaning 'dryness.' Many people use humidifiers to prevent dry skin and sore throats.
memory: Imagine dry autumn leaves becoming so brittle that they crumble in your hand. That dryness helps you remember 燥 means 'dry.'

sentence.japanese: 冬 は 空気 が 乾燥しています。
sentence.reading: ふゆ は くうき が かんそうしています。
sentence.romaji: fuyu wa kuuki ga kansou shiteimasu
sentence.english: The air is dry in winter.

fact: Japanese companies often use words such as 総合 (comprehensive), 総務 (general affairs), and 総理大臣 (Prime Minister). The kanji 総 usually refers to the whole or overall total.
memory: Imagine adding many small numbers together until you reach one grand total. That final sum helps you remember 総 means 'overall' or 'total.'

sentence.japanese: 参加者 の 総数 は 百人 でした。
sentence.reading: さんかしゃ の そうすう は ひゃくにん でした。
sentence.romaji: sankasha no sousuu wa hyakunin deshita
sentence.english: The total number of participants was one hundred.

fact: Cosplay is hugely popular in Japan, and participants often spend months creating elaborate costumes. The kanji 装 appears in words like 衣装 (costume) and 装飾 (decoration).
memory: Imagine putting on an elaborate costume before stepping onto a stage. Dressing up helps you remember 装 means 'to dress' or 'to equip.'

sentence.japanese: 店 は きれい に 装飾 されています。
sentence.reading: みせ は きれい に そうしょく されています。
sentence.romaji: mise wa kirei ni soushoku sareteimasu
sentence.english: The shop is beautifully decorated.

fact: Japan is home to the Great Buddha statues of Kamakura and Nara. The kanji 像 is used for statues, portraits, and images, and also appears in words like 映像 (video image).
memory: Imagine standing before a giant bronze Buddha statue. That impressive image helps you remember 像 means 'statue' or 'image.'

sentence.japanese: 寺 に 大きな 仏像 が あります。
sentence.reading: てら に おおきな ぶつぞう が あります。
sentence.romaji: tera ni ookina butsuzou ga arimasu
sentence.english: There is a large Buddha statue at the temple.

kunyomi: にく.む, にく.い, にく.らしい
fact: The expression 『憎めない人』 (nikumenai hito) literally means 'a person you can't hate.' It describes someone who is mischievous but still lovable.
memory: Imagine someone frowning with crossed arms because they strongly dislike something. That feeling helps you remember 憎 means 'to hate.'

sentence.japanese: 人 を 憎んでは いけません。
sentence.reading: ひと を にくんでは いけません。
sentence.romaji: hito o nikunde wa ikemasen
sentence.english: You should not hate people.

fact: The kanji 臓 appears in many medical terms, such as 心臓 (heart), 肝臓 (liver), and 腎臓 (kidney). It is commonly encountered in hospitals and health-related vocabulary.
memory: Imagine a medical diagram showing the organs inside the human body. Those internal organs help you remember 臓 means 'internal organs.'

sentence.japanese: 心臓 は とても 大切な 臓器 です。
sentence.reading: しんぞう は とても たいせつな ぞうき です。
sentence.romaji: shinzou wa totemo taisetsu na zouki desu
sentence.english: The heart is a very important organ.

fact: Traditional Japanese warehouses called 蔵 (kura) were built with thick earthen walls to protect valuable goods from fires and earthquakes. Many historic merchant towns still preserve these buildings.
memory: Imagine a sturdy warehouse filled with valuable treasures kept safely inside. That secure storehouse helps you remember 蔵 means 'storehouse.'

sentence.japanese: 野菜 は 冷蔵庫 に 入れてください。
sentence.reading: やさい は れいぞうこ に いれてください。
sentence.romaji: yasai wa reizouko ni irete kudasai
sentence.english: Please put the vegetables in the refrigerator.

fact: Gift-giving is an important part of Japanese culture. Seasonal gifts such as お歳暮 (year-end gifts) and お中元 (summer gifts) are exchanged to express gratitude and strengthen relationships.
memory: Imagine wrapping a beautiful present with a ribbon before handing it to a friend. That thoughtful gift helps you remember 贈 means 'to give as a present.'

sentence.japanese: 友達 に 誕生日 プレゼント を 贈りました。
sentence.reading: ともだち に たんじょうび プレゼント を おくりました。
sentence.romaji: tomodachi ni tanjoubi purezento o okurimashita
sentence.english: I gave my friend a birthday present.

fact: Japan is known for world-class manufacturing. The kanji 造 appears in words such as 製造 (manufacturing), 建造 (construction), and 醸造 (brewing), reflecting Japan's craftsmanship tradition.
memory: Imagine skilled craftsmen carefully building a traditional wooden temple piece by piece. That act of creating helps you remember 造 means 'to make' or 'to build.'

sentence.japanese: この 工場 は 車 を 製造しています。
sentence.reading: この こうじょう は くるま を せいぞうしています。
sentence.romaji: kono koujou wa kuruma o seizou shiteimasu
sentence.english: This factory manufactures cars.

fact: Japanese schools and companies often emphasize following rules. The word 規則 (kisoku), meaning 'rules' or 'regulations,' is one of the most common words containing this kanji.
memory: Imagine a teacher pointing to a classroom rulebook that everyone must follow. Those rules help you remember 則 means 'rule' or 'principle.'

sentence.japanese: 学校 の 規則 を 守ってください。
sentence.reading: がっこう の きそく を まもってください。
sentence.romaji: gakkou no kisoku o mamotte kudasai
sentence.english: Please follow the school rules.

fact: Japan's engineers carefully measure buildings to ensure they can withstand earthquakes. The kanji 測 appears in scientific and engineering vocabulary such as 計測 (measurement).
memory: Imagine using a measuring tape to carefully measure the length of a table. That measuring action helps you remember 測 means 'to measure.'

sentence.japanese: 長さ を 正確 に 測りました。
sentence.reading: ながさ を せいかく に はかりました。
sentence.romaji: nagasa o seikaku ni hakarimashita
sentence.english: I measured the length accurately.

fact: Graduation ceremonies are major milestones in Japan. The word 卒業 (sotsugyō), meaning 'graduation,' is one of the first words learners encounter containing this kanji.
memory: Imagine students proudly wearing graduation caps as they receive their diplomas. That ceremony helps you remember 卒 means 'graduate.'

sentence.japanese: 姉 は 去年 大学 を 卒業しました。
sentence.reading: あね は きょねん だいがく を そつぎょうしました。
sentence.romaji: ane wa kyonen daigaku o sotsugyou shimashita
sentence.english: My older sister graduated from university last year.

fact: Respect for grandparents is deeply rooted in Japanese culture. During holidays such as Obon and New Year's, many families gather so grandparents can spend time with their grandchildren (孫).
memory: Imagine a smiling grandparent happily playing with a young grandchild in the park. That warm family scene helps you remember 孫 means 'grandchild.'

sentence.japanese: 祖父 は 孫 と 遊ぶ の が 大好きです。
sentence.reading: そふ は まご と あそぶ の が だいすきです。
sentence.romaji: sofu wa mago to asobu no ga daisuki desu
sentence.english: My grandfather loves playing with his grandchild.

kunyomi: たっと.い, とうと.い, たっと.ぶ, とうと.ぶ
fact: Respect is deeply rooted in Japanese culture. The kanji 尊 appears in 尊敬 (respect) and 尊重 (respecting someone's opinions or rights), both of which are frequently used in schools and workplaces.
memory: Imagine bowing respectfully to an elderly teacher. That respectful gesture helps you remember 尊 means 'respect' or 'honor.'

sentence.japanese: 私 は 両親 を とても 尊敬しています。
sentence.reading: わたし は りょうしん を とても そんけいしています。
sentence.romaji: watashi wa ryoushin o totemo sonkei shiteimasu
sentence.english: I respect my parents very much.

kunyomi: そこ.なう, そこ.ねる
fact: The expression 損する (son suru), meaning 'to suffer a loss,' is very common in everyday Japanese. You'll also hear 損害 (damage) in insurance and legal contexts.
memory: Imagine accidentally dropping your phone and cracking the screen. That costly damage helps you remember 損 means 'loss' or 'damage.'

sentence.japanese: その 投資 で 大きな 損 を しました。
sentence.reading: その とうし で おおきな そん を しました。
sentence.romaji: sono toushi de ookina son o shimashita
sentence.english: I suffered a big loss on that investment.

fact: Traditional Japanese kimono are secured with a wide decorative belt called an 帯 (obi). Some obi are works of art, featuring intricate silk weaving and embroidery.
memory: Imagine wrapping a beautiful obi around a colorful kimono. That belt helps you remember 帯 means 'belt' or 'to wear around the waist.'

sentence.japanese: 携帯電話 を 忘れました。
sentence.reading: けいたいでんわ を わすれました。
sentence.romaji: keitaidenwa o wasuremashita
sentence.english: I forgot my mobile phone.

fact: The kanji 替 appears in everyday situations, from changing trains (乗り換える) to replacing batteries. It is also found in 為替 (kawase), meaning 'exchange' as in foreign exchange.
memory: Imagine removing an old light bulb and replacing it with a new one. That exchange helps you remember 替 means 'replace.'

sentence.japanese: 家 に 帰って 服 を 着替えました。
sentence.reading: いえ に かえって ふく を きがえました。
sentence.romaji: ie ni kaette fuku o kigaemashita
sentence.english: I changed my clothes after returning home.

fact: Since Japan introduced charges for plastic shopping bags in many stores, the word 袋 (fukuro) has become even more common in daily conversations. Cashiers often ask 『袋はご利用ですか？』 ('Do you need a bag?').
memory: Imagine carrying groceries home in a large paper bag. That shopping bag helps you remember 袋 means 'bag.'

sentence.japanese: 買った 本 を 袋 に 入れました。
sentence.reading: かった ほん を ふくろ に いれました。
sentence.romaji: katta hon o fukuro ni iremashita
sentence.english: I put the book into a bag.

fact: The kanji 濯 is almost always seen in the word 洗濯 (sentaku), meaning 'laundry.' In Japan, many people hang clothes outside to dry, making laundry an everyday part of neighborhood scenery.
memory: Imagine rinsing freshly washed clothes in clean water before hanging them in the sun. That rinsing action helps you remember 濯 means 'to wash' or 'to rinse.'

sentence.japanese: 今日は 洗濯 を しました。
sentence.reading: きょう は せんたく を しました。
sentence.romaji: kyou wa sentaku o shimashita
sentence.english: I did the laundry today.

fact: Many Japanese place names contain the kanji 谷 (valley), including the famous Shibuya district (渋谷). Japan's mountainous landscape has made valleys an important part of its geography.
memory: Imagine standing between two tall mountains with a river flowing through the valley below. That landscape helps you remember 谷 means 'valley.'

sentence.japanese: 美しい 谷 を 散歩しました。
sentence.reading: うつくしい たに を さんぽしました。
sentence.romaji: utsukushii tani o sanpo shimashita
sentence.english: I took a walk through a beautiful valley.

fact: In Japanese companies, people are often referred to as 担当者 (tantōsha), meaning 'the person in charge.' The kanji 担 is strongly associated with responsibility and assigned duties.
memory: Imagine carrying a heavy backpack on your shoulders because you're responsible for delivering it. That burden helps you remember 担 means 'to carry' or 'to bear responsibility.'

sentence.japanese: 営業 の 担当者 と 話しました。
sentence.reading: えいぎょう の たんとうしゃ と はなしました。
sentence.romaji: eigyou no tantousha to hanashimashita
sentence.english: I spoke with the sales representative.

fact: Many organizations in Japan use 団 in their names, such as 劇団 (theater company) and 財団 (foundation). It commonly refers to organized groups of people.
memory: Imagine a sports team standing together for a group photo before a match. That united team helps you remember 団 means 'group.'

sentence.japanese: その 団体 は 環境保護 活動 を しています。
sentence.reading: その だんたい は かんきょうほご かつどう を しています。
sentence.romaji: sono dantai wa kankyouhogo katsudou o shiteimasu
sentence.english: That organization works to protect the environment.

fact: The kanji 築 appears in words such as 建築 (architecture) and 築地 (Tsukiji). The famous Tsukiji Market in Tokyo was one of Japan's best-known seafood markets for decades.
memory: Imagine construction workers laying bricks one by one to build a strong house. That careful construction helps you remember 築 means 'to build.'

sentence.japanese: 彼 は 長年 の 信頼 を 築きました。
sentence.reading: かれ は ながねん の しんらい を きずきました。
sentence.romaji: kare wa naganen no shinrai o kizukimashita
sentence.english: He built trust over many years.

fact: Hokkaido is Japan's largest dairy-producing region. Words such as 畜産 (livestock farming) are commonly used when discussing agriculture and food production.
memory: Imagine a farmer feeding cows and sheep in a large pasture every morning. Those farm animals help you remember 畜 means 'livestock.'

sentence.japanese: 北海道 は 畜産 が 盛んです。
sentence.reading: ほっかいどう は ちくさん が さかんです。
sentence.romaji: Hokkaidou wa chikusan ga sakan desu
sentence.english: Livestock farming is thriving in Hokkaido.

fact: Bamboo forests, such as the famous Arashiyama Bamboo Grove in Kyoto, are iconic landscapes in Japan. Bamboo is also used to make chopsticks, baskets, and traditional musical instruments.
memory: Imagine walking through a tall bamboo forest with green stalks swaying in the wind. That forest helps you remember 竹 means 'bamboo.'

sentence.japanese: 京都 の 竹林 は とても 美しいです。
sentence.reading: きょうと の ちくりん は とても うつくしいです。
sentence.romaji: Kyouto no chikurin wa totemo utsukushii desu
sentence.english: Kyoto's bamboo grove is very beautiful.

fact: The expression 仲良し (nakayoshi), meaning 'close friends,' is commonly heard among children and adults alike. Maintaining good relationships is highly valued in Japanese society.
memory: Imagine two close friends walking side by side and laughing together. Their friendship helps you remember 仲 means 'relationship' or 'companion.'

sentence.japanese: 私たち は とても 仲 が 良いです。
sentence.reading: わたしたち は とても なか が よいです。
sentence.romaji: watashitachi wa totemo naka ga yoi desu
sentence.english: We get along very well.

fact: Traditional Japanese wooden temples often rely on massive wooden pillars that have allowed some buildings to survive for over a thousand years. 柱 is also the counter for gods in Shinto mythology.
memory: Imagine a huge wooden pillar holding up the roof of an ancient temple. That strong support helps you remember 柱 means 'pillar.'

sentence.japanese: 古い 家 の 柱 は とても 太いです。
sentence.reading: ふるい いえ の はしら は とても ふといです。
sentence.romaji: furui ie no hashira wa totemo futoi desu
sentence.english: The pillars of the old house are very thick.

fact: Cicadas (蝉) are famous symbols of Japanese summer. Their loud songs fill parks and forests every year, making insects an important part of Japan's seasonal culture.
memory: Imagine a tiny bug crawling across a green leaf. That little insect helps you remember 虫 means 'bug' or 'insect.'

sentence.japanese: 夏 は 虫 が 多いです。
sentence.reading: なつ は むし が おおいです。
sentence.romaji: natsu wa mushi ga ooi desu
sentence.english: There are many insects in summer.

fact: You'll see this kanji almost everywhere in Japan on parking signs such as 駐車場 (parking lot) and 駐車禁止 (No Parking). It originally meant 'to remain stationed' in one place.
memory: Imagine a car remaining in one parking space all day without moving. Staying in one place helps you remember 駐 means 'to park' or 'to be stationed.'

sentence.japanese: ここ に 車 を 駐車しては いけません。
sentence.reading: ここ に くるま を ちゅうしゃしては いけません。
sentence.romaji: koko ni kuruma o chuusha shite wa ikemasen
sentence.english: You must not park your car here.

fact: Japan has one of the world's highest household savings rates. The kanji 貯 appears in everyday financial words like 貯金 (savings) and 貯蓄 (saving money).
memory: Imagine dropping coins into a piggy bank every day until it becomes full. Saving those coins helps you remember 貯 means 'to save.'

sentence.japanese: 毎月 お金 を 貯めています。
sentence.reading: まいつき おかね を ためています。
sentence.romaji: maitsuki okane o tameteimasu
sentence.english: I save money every month.

fact: In Japanese numbering, 1兆 equals one trillion (1,000,000,000,000). The kanji is also used in words referring to signs or indications, such as 前兆 (omen).
memory: Imagine seeing dark clouds gathering as a sign that a storm is coming. That omen helps you remember 兆 means 'sign' or 'trillion.'

sentence.japanese: 景気 回復 の 兆し が 見えます。
sentence.reading: けいき かいふく の きざし が みえます。
sentence.romaji: keiki kaifuku no kizashi ga miemasu
sentence.english: There are signs of an economic recovery.

fact: Many important Japanese government agencies end with 庁, including 気象庁 (Japan Meteorological Agency) and 消防庁 (Fire and Disaster Management Agency).
memory: Imagine a government office building where officials work to serve the public. That government agency helps you remember 庁 means 'government office.'

sentence.japanese: その 手続き は 市役所 ではなく 官庁 で 行います。
sentence.reading: その てつづき は しやくしょ ではなく かんちょう で おこないます。
sentence.romaji: sono tetsuzuki wa shiyakusho dewa naku kanchou de okonaimasu
sentence.english: That procedure is handled at a government office, not city hall.

fact: Modern Japanese uses 超 as an informal intensifier, much like 'super' in English. Young people often say 『超かわいい！』 ('Super cute!') or 『超おいしい！』 ('Super delicious!').
memory: Imagine a superhero leaping over a huge wall in a single jump. Going beyond the limit helps you remember 超 means 'to exceed' or 'super.'

sentence.japanese: 気温 は 三十度 を 超えました。
sentence.reading: きおん は さんじゅうど を こえました。
sentence.romaji: kion wa sanjuudo o koemashita
sentence.english: The temperature exceeded 30 degrees.

fact: The word 沈黙 (chinmoku), meaning 'silence,' is common in Japanese. Just as an object sinks beneath the water, a conversation can 'sink' into complete silence.
memory: Imagine a small boat slowly sinking beneath the surface of a lake until it disappears. That image helps you remember 沈 means 'to sink.'

sentence.japanese: 船 が 海 に 沈みました。
sentence.reading: ふね が うみ に しずみました。
sentence.romaji: fune ga umi ni shizumimashita
sentence.english: The ship sank into the sea.

fact: Japan has many specialty foods known as 珍味 (chinmi), literally 'rare tastes.' These unusual delicacies are often regional specialties enjoyed by adventurous diners.
memory: Imagine discovering a rare jewel that almost nobody has ever seen. That unusual treasure helps you remember 珍 means 'rare.'

sentence.japanese: これは とても 珍しい 花 です。
sentence.reading: これ は とても めずらしい はな です。
sentence.romaji: kore wa totemo mezurashii hana desu
sentence.english: This is a very rare flower.

fact: Bus stops (停留所) and train stops across Japan use this kanji. It commonly refers to stopping or remaining at a fixed location.
memory: Imagine a bus stopping at a bus stop so passengers can get on and off. That stop helps you remember 停 means 'to stop.'

sentence.japanese: 電車 は 次 の 駅 で 停車します。
sentence.reading: でんしゃ は つぎ の えき で ていしゃします。
sentence.romaji: densha wa tsugi no eki de teisha shimasu
sentence.english: The train will stop at the next station.

fact: The deepest part of the ocean is called 海底 (kaitei), meaning 'the seabed.' Japan's surrounding seas make this kanji common in geography and science.
memory: Imagine looking through clear water until you can finally see the bottom of a lake. That lowest point helps you remember 底 means 'bottom.'

sentence.japanese: 湖 の 底 が 見えます。
sentence.reading: みずうみ の そこ が みえます。
sentence.romaji: mizuumi no soko ga miemasu
sentence.english: I can see the bottom of the lake.

fact: Rice farming has been part of Japanese culture for thousands of years. Farmers traditionally plant rice seedlings by hand while standing ankle-deep in muddy fields.
memory: Imagine stepping into thick mud after heavy rain and struggling to pull your boots free. That sticky mud helps you remember 泥 means 'mud.'

sentence.japanese: 靴 が 泥 で 汚れました。
sentence.reading: くつ が どろ で よごれました。
sentence.romaji: kutsu ga doro de yogoremashita
sentence.english: My shoes got dirty with mud.

fact: The word 水滴 (suiteki), meaning 'water droplet,' is commonly used during Japan's rainy season (梅雨). Morning dew on leaves is also described using this kanji.
memory: Imagine a single raindrop slowly falling from the tip of a leaf. That tiny droplet helps you remember 滴 means 'drop' or 'drip.'

sentence.japanese: 葉 から 水滴 が 落ちました。
sentence.reading: は から すいてき が おちました。
sentence.romaji: ha kara suiteki ga ochimashita
sentence.english: A drop of water fell from the leaf.

fact: Japan's railway network is so famous that the word 鉄道 (railway) is instantly recognized across the country. Train enthusiasts are even nicknamed 鉄道ファン or simply 鉄ちゃん.
memory: Imagine a heavy iron beam supporting a bridge. That strong metal helps you remember 鉄 means 'iron.'

sentence.japanese: 地下鉄 で 会社 へ 行きます。
sentence.reading: ちかてつ で かいしゃ へ いきます。
sentence.romaji: chikatetsu de kaisha e ikimasu
sentence.english: I go to work by subway.

fact: Even today, the honorific ～殿 (dono) is sometimes used in formal government documents and business correspondence. Historically, it was a respectful title for feudal lords.
memory: Imagine a powerful feudal lord standing proudly in his grand palace. That noble lord helps you remember 殿 means 'lord' or 'palace.'

sentence.japanese: 神社 の 本殿 は とても 美しいです。
sentence.reading: じんじゃ の ほんでん は とても うつくしいです。
sentence.romaji: jinja no honden wa totemo utsukushii desu
sentence.english: The main shrine building is very beautiful.

fact: Japan is famous for 漆塗り (urushi-nuri), the traditional art of coating wood with natural lacquer. Beautiful lacquerware has been produced for centuries.
memory: Imagine carefully painting a wooden table with a brush until every part is covered. That coating helps you remember 塗 means 'to paint.'

sentence.japanese: 壁 を 白く 塗りました。
sentence.reading: かべ を しろく ぬりました。
sentence.romaji: kabe o shiroku nurimashita
sentence.english: I painted the wall white.

fact: Japan has a multi-party political system. News reports frequently mention 政党 (political parties), such as the Liberal Democratic Party (自民党).
memory: Imagine politicians standing together under the same party flag during an election campaign. That united group helps you remember 党 means 'political party.'

sentence.japanese: 選挙 の 前 に 各 政党 が 公約 を 発表しました。
sentence.reading: せんきょ の まえ に かく せいとう が こうやく を はっぴょうしました。
sentence.romaji: senkyo no mae ni kaku seitou ga kouyaku o happyou shimashita
sentence.english: Before the election, each political party announced its campaign promises.

kunyomi: こお.る, こご.える, こお.らせる
fact: Every winter, northern Japan hosts spectacular ice festivals such as the Sapporo Snow Festival, where frozen sculptures and ice buildings attract millions of visitors. The kanji 凍 appears in many winter-related words.
memory: Imagine leaving a bottle of water outside on a freezing night until it turns into solid ice. That transformation helps you remember 凍 means 'to freeze.'

sentence.japanese: 冬 は 湖 が 凍ります。
sentence.reading: ふゆ は みずうみ が こおります。
sentence.romaji: fuyu wa mizuumi ga koorimasu
sentence.english: The lake freezes in winter.

fact: Japan's famous five-story pagodas, such as those at Hōryū-ji and Tō-ji, have survived earthquakes for centuries thanks to their ingenious wooden architecture.
memory: Imagine looking up at a tall pagoda reaching toward the sky. That towering structure helps you remember 塔 means 'tower.'

sentence.japanese: 東京タワー は 人気 の 観光地 です。
sentence.reading: とうきょうタワー は にんき の かんこうち です。
sentence.romaji: Toukyou Tawaa wa ninki no kankouchi desu
sentence.english: Tokyo Tower is a popular tourist attraction.

fact: Japanese bathing culture revolves around hot water. Public baths (銭湯) and hot springs (温泉) are beloved places for relaxation throughout the country.
memory: Imagine relaxing in a steaming hot bath after a long day. That warm water helps you remember 湯 means 'hot water.'

sentence.japanese: お湯 を 入れて お茶 を 飲みます。
sentence.reading: おゆ を いれて おちゃ を のみます。
sentence.romaji: oyu o irete ocha o nomimasu
sentence.english: I add hot water and drink tea.

kunyomi: ひ, ともしび, とも.す
fact: Traditional stone lanterns (石灯籠) decorate the paths of many Japanese temples and gardens. Their soft light creates a peaceful atmosphere, especially at night.
memory: Imagine lighting a lantern that glows warmly in the darkness. That gentle light helps you remember 灯 means 'lamp' or 'light.'

sentence.japanese: 夜 に 街灯 が 点きます。
sentence.reading: よる に がいとう が つきます。
sentence.romaji: yoru ni gaitou ga tsukimasu
sentence.english: The streetlights turn on at night.

fact: Bamboo tubes have been used in Japan for centuries to carry water, store tea, and even make musical instruments. The kanji 筒 often refers to tube-shaped objects.
memory: Imagine holding a hollow bamboo tube that you can look through like a telescope. That cylindrical shape helps you remember 筒 means 'tube.'

sentence.japanese: 手紙 を 封筒 に 入れました。
sentence.reading: てがみ を ふうとう に いれました。
sentence.romaji: tegami o fuutou ni iremashita
sentence.english: I put the letter into an envelope.

fact: Tour guides in Japan are called ガイド or 案内人, but the kanji 導 appears in many important words such as 指導 (guidance) and 導入 (introduction). Teachers, coaches, and managers all provide 指導 to help others improve.
memory: Imagine holding a lantern while leading travelers safely through a dark forest. Guiding others along the correct path helps you remember 導 means 'to lead' or 'to guide.'

sentence.japanese: 先生 が 私たち を 正しい 方向 に 導いてくれました。
sentence.reading: せんせい が わたしたち を ただしい ほうこう に みちびいてくれました。
sentence.romaji: sensei ga watashitachi o tadashii houkou ni michibiite kuremashita
sentence.english: The teacher guided us in the right direction.

fact: The famous Japanese folktale character 金太郎 (Kintarō) is often depicted as a strong young boy. The kanji 童 appears in words like 児童 (children) and 童話 (fairy tale).
memory: Imagine a cheerful child happily running through a playground. That playful youngster helps you remember 童 means 'child.'

sentence.japanese: その 本 は 児童 向けです。
sentence.reading: その ほん は じどう むけです。
sentence.romaji: sono hon wa jidou muke desu
sentence.english: That book is intended for children.

fact: The Great Buddha of Nara is made primarily of bronze, an alloy containing copper. The kanji 銅 appears in words related to metals, coins, and Olympic medals (銅メダル).
memory: Imagine a shiny reddish metal coin made of copper. That distinctive color helps you remember 銅 means 'copper.'

sentence.japanese: 彼 は 銅メダル を 獲得しました。
sentence.reading: かれ は どうメダル を かくとくしました。
sentence.romaji: kare wa dou medaru o kakutoku shimashita
sentence.english: He won the bronze medal.

fact: Japan is home to the poisonous pufferfish (ふぐ). Only licensed chefs are allowed to prepare it because improperly prepared fugu contains deadly poison.
memory: Imagine a bottle with a skull-and-crossbones warning label. That dangerous poison helps you remember 毒 means 'poison.'

sentence.japanese: その 植物 は 毒 が あります。
sentence.reading: その しょくぶつ は どく が あります。
sentence.romaji: sono shokubutsu wa doku ga arimasu
sentence.english: That plant is poisonous.

fact: Japan's delivery services are famous for their speed and reliability. The word 届ける is commonly used when packages, letters, or meals are delivered to homes.
memory: Imagine a delivery driver handing a package directly to your front door. That successful delivery helps you remember 届 means 'to deliver' or 'to reach.'

sentence.japanese: 荷物 が 今日 届きました。
sentence.reading: にもつ が きょう とどきました。
sentence.romaji: nimotsu ga kyou todokimashita
sentence.english: The package arrived today.

kunyomi: くも.る, くも.り, くも.らす
fact: Japanese weather forecasts often use the symbol ☁ and the word 曇り (kumori) to indicate cloudy weather. During the rainy season (梅雨), this kanji appears frequently in forecasts.
memory: Imagine dark gray clouds slowly covering the bright blue sky until the sun disappears. That overcast sky helps you remember 曇 means 'cloudy.'

sentence.japanese: 今日 は 一日中 曇り でした。
sentence.reading: きょう は いちにちじゅう くもり でした。
sentence.romaji: kyou wa ichinichijuu kumori deshita
sentence.english: It was cloudy all day today.

kunyomi: にぶ.い, にぶ.る, なま.る
fact: The word 鈍感 (donkan), meaning 'insensitive' or 'oblivious,' is commonly used in manga, anime, and everyday conversation to describe someone who doesn't notice obvious things.
memory: Imagine trying to cut paper with a dull knife that barely works. That blunt blade helps you remember 鈍 means 'dull' or 'slow.'

sentence.japanese: この ナイフ は 少し 鈍いです。
sentence.reading: この ナイフ は すこし にぶいです。
sentence.romaji: kono naifu wa sukoshi nibui desu
sentence.english: This knife is a little dull.

fact: The word 軟式 (nanshiki), meaning 'soft-style,' is used in Japanese sports such as baseball, where a softer rubber ball is used instead of a hard one.
memory: Imagine squeezing a soft rubber ball that easily changes shape in your hand. That softness helps you remember 軟 means 'soft' or 'flexible.'

sentence.japanese: この パン は とても 軟らかいです。
sentence.reading: この パン は とても やわらかいです。
sentence.romaji: kono pan wa totemo yawarakai desu
sentence.english: This bread is very soft.

fact: Hokkaido produces more than half of Japan's milk. The kanji 乳 appears in many dairy-related words, including 牛乳 (milk) and 乳製品 (dairy products).
memory: Imagine pouring fresh milk into a glass at breakfast. That creamy milk helps you remember 乳 means 'milk.'

sentence.japanese: 毎朝 牛乳 を 飲みます。
sentence.reading: まいあさ ぎゅうにゅう を のみます。
sentence.romaji: maiasa gyuunyuu o nomimasu
sentence.english: I drink milk every morning.

kunyomi: も.える, も.やす, も.す
fact: Japan has strict garbage-sorting rules. Burnable trash is labeled 燃えるごみ, while non-burnable trash is labeled 燃えないごみ.
memory: Imagine a campfire with bright orange flames rising into the night sky. That burning fire helps you remember 燃 means 'to burn.'

sentence.japanese: 紙 が よく 燃えます。
sentence.reading: かみ が よく もえます。
sentence.romaji: kami ga yoku moemasu
sentence.english: Paper burns easily.

fact: The phrase 悩み相談 (nayami sōdan), meaning 'consultation about worries,' is commonly used in Japan for counseling services, advice columns, and radio programs where people discuss personal problems.
memory: Imagine someone sitting alone with their head in their hands, thinking deeply about a difficult problem. That troubled expression helps you remember 悩 means 'to worry.'

sentence.japanese: 彼 は 将来 の こと を 悩んでいます。
sentence.reading: かれ は しょうらい の こと を なやんでいます。
sentence.romaji: kare wa shourai no koto o nayandeimasu
sentence.english: He is worried about his future.

fact: Japanese tea lovers often describe strong green tea as 濃いお茶 (koi ocha). The kanji 濃 is also widely used for concentrated flavors, dark colors, and dense fog.
memory: Imagine pouring a cup of dark, strong green tea with a rich flavor. That concentrated tea helps you remember 濃 means 'thick' or 'concentrated.'

sentence.japanese: この コーヒー は 少し 濃いです。
sentence.reading: この コーヒー は すこし こいです。
sentence.romaji: kono koohii wa sukoshi koi desu
sentence.english: This coffee is a little strong.

fact: The kanji 脳 appears in many medical and scientific terms such as 脳科学 (neuroscience) and 脳卒中 (stroke). It is one of the most important body-related kanji in advanced Japanese.
memory: Imagine looking at a detailed model of the human brain filled with millions of connections. That amazing organ helps you remember 脳 means 'brain.'

sentence.japanese: 脳 は 人体 の とても 大切な 器官 です。
sentence.reading: のう は じんたい の とても たいせつな きかん です。
sentence.romaji: nou wa jintai no totemo taisetsu na kikan desu
sentence.english: The brain is a very important organ in the human body.

fact: The world-famous artwork 'The Great Wave off Kanagawa' (神奈川沖浪裏) is one of Japan's most recognizable images. Waves have long been an important symbol in Japanese art and culture.
memory: Imagine a huge ocean wave rising high before crashing onto the shore. That powerful wave helps you remember 波 means 'wave.'

sentence.japanese: 波 の 音 が 聞こえます。
sentence.reading: なみ の おと が きこえます。
sentence.romaji: nami no oto ga kikoemasu
sentence.english: I can hear the sound of the waves.

fact: When visiting a Shinto shrine, worshippers typically bow twice, clap twice, and bow once more. The kanji 拝 appears in words such as 参拝 (visiting a shrine) and 礼拝 (worship).
memory: Imagine respectfully bowing with your hands together in front of a shrine. That act of reverence helps you remember 拝 means 'to worship' or 'to bow.'

sentence.japanese: 初詣 で 神社 を 参拝しました。
sentence.reading: はつもうで で じんじゃ を さんぱいしました。
sentence.romaji: hatsumoude de jinja o sanpai shimashita
sentence.english: I visited a shrine for New Year's prayers.

fact: Japan often uses 倍 to describe discounts and reward points. During shopping campaigns, stores may advertise ポイント2倍 ('double reward points'), making this kanji common in advertisements.
memory: Imagine two identical stacks of coins sitting side by side. Having twice as many coins helps you remember 倍 means 'double.'

sentence.japanese: 今年 の 売上 は 去年 の 二倍 に なりました。
sentence.reading: ことし の うりあげ は きょねん の にばい に なりました。
sentence.romaji: kotoshi no uriage wa kyonen no nibai ni narimashita
sentence.english: This year's sales became double last year's.

fact: Traditional Japanese inns are called 旅館 (ryokan). Reservations often indicate the number of nights using 泊, such as 一泊二日 ('one night, two days').
memory: Imagine spending the night at a cozy ryokan with a hot spring bath. Staying overnight helps you remember 泊 means 'to stay.'

sentence.japanese: 京都 の 旅館 に 一泊しました。
sentence.reading: きょうと の りょかん に いっぱくしました。
sentence.romaji: Kyouto no ryokan ni ippaku shimashita
sentence.english: I stayed one night at a ryokan in Kyoto.

kunyomi: うす.い, うす.まる, うす.める
fact: Japanese people often describe light tea or weak coffee as 薄い. The kanji also describes thin objects, pale colors, and weak concentrations.
memory: Imagine adding too much water to juice until the flavor becomes weak and thin. That diluted drink helps you remember 薄 means 'thin' or 'dilute.'

sentence.japanese: この スープ は 少し 薄いです。
sentence.reading: この スープ は すこし うすいです。
sentence.romaji: kono suupu wa sukoshi usui desu
sentence.english: This soup is a little bland.

fact: Modern Japanese often uses 爆 as slang to mean 'huge' or 'massive,' such as 爆売れ ('selling extremely well') or 爆笑 ('bursting into laughter').
memory: Imagine fireworks exploding across the night sky with a loud BOOM. That powerful explosion helps you remember 爆 means 'explosion.'

sentence.japanese: ガス が 爆発しました。
sentence.reading: ガス が ばくはつしました。
sentence.romaji: gasu ga bakuhatsu shimashita
sentence.english: The gas exploded.

fact: Japanese skincare products are famous worldwide. Expressions like 美肌 (beautiful skin) and 肌荒れ (rough skin) are commonly seen in cosmetics advertisements.
memory: Imagine gently touching the smooth skin on the back of your hand. That soft feeling helps you remember 肌 means 'skin.'

sentence.japanese: 冬 は 肌 が 乾燥しやすいです。
sentence.reading: ふゆ は はだ が かんそうしやすいです。
sentence.romaji: fuyu wa hada ga kansou shiyasui desu
sentence.english: Skin dries out easily in winter.

fact: Wood has been one of Japan's most important building materials for centuries. Traditional homes, temples, and shrines often use thick wooden boards (板) for floors, walls, and signs.
memory: Imagine standing on a long wooden plank that forms part of a bridge. That wooden board helps you remember 板 means 'board' or 'plank.'

sentence.japanese: 店 の 看板 が 新しく なりました。
sentence.reading: みせ の かんばん が あたらしく なりました。
sentence.romaji: mise no kanban ga atarashiku narimashita
sentence.english: The shop's signboard became new.

fact: The kanji 版 is widely used in modern Japanese for software and books. For example, 日本語版 means 'Japanese version,' while 初版 refers to the first edition of a book.
memory: Imagine a printing press producing a new edition of your favorite book. That printed version helps you remember 版 means 'edition.'

sentence.japanese: 最新版 の ソフト を ダウンロードしました。
sentence.reading: さいしんばん の ソフト を ダウンロードしました。
sentence.romaji: saishinban no sofuto o daunroodo shimashita
sentence.english: I downloaded the latest version of the software.

fact: The kanji 般 is most commonly seen in compounds such as 一般 (general) and 一般的 (general, typical). It often describes things that apply to the public or to people in general.
memory: Imagine a large crowd representing ordinary people from every walk of life. That general public helps you remember 般 means 'general.'

sentence.japanese: この 情報 は 一般 に 公開されています。
sentence.reading: この じょうほう は いっぱん に こうかいされています。
sentence.romaji: kono jouhou wa ippan ni koukai sareteimasu
sentence.english: This information is open to the public.

fact: Japan is famous for its vending machines, which are called 自動販売機 (jidōhanbaiki). The kanji 販 appears in many words related to selling products and retail businesses.
memory: Imagine a vending machine selling drinks all day long. That act of selling helps you remember 販 means 'sell.'

sentence.japanese: この 店 は 野菜 を 販売しています。
sentence.reading: この みせ は やさい を はんばいしています。
sentence.romaji: kono mise wa yasai o hanbai shiteimasu
sentence.english: This shop sells vegetables.

fact: The kanji 比 appears frequently in mathematics, science, and statistics. Words like 比較 (comparison) and 比率 (ratio) are commonly used in business reports and research.
memory: Imagine placing two objects side by side to see which one is larger. Comparing them helps you remember 比 means 'to compare.'

sentence.japanese: 二つ の 商品 を 比較しました。
sentence.reading: ふたつ の しょうひん を ひかくしました。
sentence.romaji: futatsu no shouhin o hikaku shimashita
sentence.english: I compared the two products.

fact: The kanji 被 is very common in news reports. Words like 被害 (damage), 被災 (disaster victim), and 被告 (defendant) frequently appear in newspapers and television broadcasts.
memory: Imagine someone wearing a helmet while suffering through a heavy storm. The idea of being 'affected by' something helps you remember 被 means 'to suffer' or 'to receive.'

sentence.japanese: 台風 で 大きな 被害 が 出ました。
sentence.reading: たいふう で おおきな ひがい が でました。
sentence.romaji: taifuu de ookina higai ga demashita
sentence.english: The typhoon caused major damage.

fact: Spring in Japan brings beautiful cherry blossoms—and pollen. Many people suffer from 花粉症 (hay fever), making words related to the nose, like 鼻水 (runny nose), especially common.
memory: Imagine sneezing because cherry blossom pollen tickles your nose. That itchy nose helps you remember 鼻 means 'nose.'

sentence.japanese: 風邪 で 鼻 が 詰まっています。
sentence.reading: かぜ で はな が つまっています。
sentence.romaji: kaze de hana ga tsumatteimasu
sentence.english: My nose is blocked because of a cold.

fact: Japanese uses different counters for different objects. 匹 is the standard counter for small animals such as cats, dogs, fish, rabbits, and insects.
memory: Imagine counting three playful puppies: one puppy, two puppies, three puppies. Counting small animals helps you remember 匹 is the counter for them.

sentence.japanese: 公園 で 猫 を 二匹 見ました。
sentence.reading: こうえん で ねこ を にひき みました。
sentence.romaji: kouen de neko o nihiki mimashita
sentence.english: I saw two cats in the park.

fact: Japanese calligraphy (書道) is traditionally practiced with a brush called a 筆. Master calligraphers spend years learning proper brush control and stroke order.
memory: Imagine dipping a calligraphy brush into black ink before writing beautiful kanji. That writing brush helps you remember 筆 means 'brush.'

sentence.japanese: 書道 で 筆 を 使います。
sentence.reading: しょどう で ふで を つかいます。
sentence.romaji: shodou de fude o tsukaimasu
sentence.english: We use a brush in Japanese calligraphy.

fact: During Japan's hot summers, many people enjoy かき氷 (shaved ice), a popular dessert topped with colorful fruit syrups, matcha, or sweet condensed milk.
memory: Imagine holding a cold block of ice that slowly melts in your hands. That frozen ice helps you remember 氷 means 'ice.'

sentence.japanese: 飲み物 に 氷 を 入れました。
sentence.reading: のみもの に こおり を いれました。
sentence.romaji: nomimono ni koori o iremashita
sentence.english: I put ice in my drink.

fact: Japan's famous Shinkansen trains are known for incredible punctuality. Their average delays are often measured in mere seconds (秒), making this kanji common in news and transportation.
memory: Imagine watching a stopwatch tick one second at a time. Each tiny tick helps you remember 秒 means 'second.'

sentence.japanese: あと 十秒 待ってください。
sentence.reading: あと じゅうびょう まってください。
sentence.romaji: ato juubyou matte kudasai
sentence.english: Please wait another ten seconds.

fact: Glass milk bottles (牛乳瓶) were once delivered to many Japanese homes each morning. Even today, some traditional hot spring inns serve milk in classic glass bottles after a bath.
memory: Imagine holding a glass bottle filled with fresh milk. That bottle helps you remember 瓶 means 'bottle.'

sentence.japanese: 瓶 に 水 を 入れました。
sentence.reading: びん に みず を いれました。
sentence.romaji: bin ni mizu o iremashita
sentence.english: I poured water into the bottle.

fact: Japan has a long tradition of beautiful textiles, from kimono silk to indigo-dyed cotton. The kanji 布 appears in many words related to fabric and distribution.
memory: Imagine touching a soft piece of cloth used to make a traditional kimono. That fabric helps you remember 布 means 'cloth.'

sentence.japanese: 寒いので 毛布 を 使いました。
sentence.reading: さむいので もうふ を つかいました。
sentence.romaji: samui node moufu o tsukaimashita
sentence.english: I used a blanket because it was cold.

fact: Japan has only two prefectures that use 府 instead of 県: 京都府 (Kyoto Prefecture) and 大阪府 (Osaka Prefecture). This makes 府 a distinctive administrative kanji.
memory: Imagine a government office building with the signs 'Kyoto Prefecture' and 'Osaka Prefecture' outside. That government office helps you remember 府 means 'urban prefecture.'

sentence.japanese: 大阪府 に 住んでいます。
sentence.reading: おおさかふ に すんでいます。
sentence.romaji: Oosaka-fu ni sundeimasu
sentence.english: I live in Osaka Prefecture.

fact: The kanji 普 appears in very common words such as 普通 (normal), 普及 (spread), and 普遍 (universal). You'll see 普通 on trains to indicate a local train that stops at every station.
memory: Imagine an ordinary local train stopping at every station because it serves everyone. That idea of being common or universal helps you remember 普 means 'general.'

sentence.japanese: 今日は 普通 電車 で 帰ります。
sentence.reading: きょう は ふつう でんしゃ で かえります。
sentence.romaji: kyou wa futsuu densha de kaerimasu
sentence.english: I'm going home by the local train today.

fact: Train tickets in Japan often include the kanji 切符 (kippu), one of the most common words containing 符. Although IC cards are widespread today, the word 切符 is still used every day.
memory: Imagine holding a train ticket with a special symbol printed on it. That ticket helps you remember 符 means 'symbol' or 'token.'

sentence.japanese: 駅 で 切符 を 買いました。
sentence.reading: えき で きっぷ を かいました。
sentence.romaji: eki de kippu o kaimashita
sentence.english: I bought a train ticket at the station.

fact: The kanji 膚 almost always appears in compound words such as 皮膚 (skin). It is commonly seen in hospitals, pharmacies, and skincare products throughout Japan.
memory: Imagine a doctor examining healthy skin under a bright light. That smooth skin helps you remember 膚 means 'skin.'

sentence.japanese: 皮膚 が 乾燥しています。
sentence.reading: ひふ が かんそうしています。
sentence.romaji: hifu ga kansou shiteimasu
sentence.english: My skin is dry.

fact: The word 武士 (bushi), meaning 'samurai,' comes from this kanji. It also appears in 武道 (martial arts), which includes disciplines like judo, kendo, and karate.
memory: Imagine a samurai standing proudly with a sword, ready to protect his village. That warrior helps you remember 武 means 'military' or 'warrior.'

sentence.japanese: 弟 は 武道 を 習っています。
sentence.reading: おとうと は ぶどう を ならっています。
sentence.romaji: otouto wa budou o naratteimasu
sentence.english: My younger brother is learning martial arts.

fact: Letters and official documents are often placed inside 封筒 (envelopes). The kanji 封 also appears in words related to sealing, closing, and restricting access.
memory: Imagine sealing an envelope before mailing an important letter. That sealed envelope helps you remember 封 means 'to seal.'

sentence.japanese: 手紙 を 封筒 に 入れて 封をしました。
sentence.reading: てがみ を ふうとう に いれて ふう を しました。
sentence.romaji: tegami o fuutou ni irete fuu o shimashita
sentence.english: I put the letter in an envelope and sealed it.

fact: Many Japanese companies and organizations use 副 in job titles, such as 副社長 (vice president) and 副部長 (assistant department manager).
memory: Imagine a vice president standing beside the company president, ready to help. That supporting role helps you remember 副 means 'vice' or 'assistant.'

sentence.japanese: この 薬 は 副作用 が 少ないです。
sentence.reading: この くすり は ふくさよう が すくないです。
sentence.romaji: kono kusuri wa fukusayou ga sukunai desu
sentence.english: This medicine has few side effects.

fact: The kanji 復 appears in many everyday words such as 復習 (review), 回復 (recovery), and 往復 (round trip). Students in Japan constantly hear '復習しましょう' after class.
memory: Imagine retracing your footsteps back home after forgetting your wallet. Returning to the starting point helps you remember 復 means 'return' or 'recover.'

sentence.japanese: 試験 の 前 に 復習 を しました。
sentence.reading: しけん の まえ に ふくしゅう を しました。
sentence.romaji: shiken no mae ni fukushuu o shimashita
sentence.english: I reviewed before the exam.

fact: Japan's railway system uses the word 軌道幅 (track gauge) when referring to the distance between train rails. The kanji 幅 commonly refers to physical width or range.
memory: Stretch your arms wide to measure the width of a doorway. That distance from one side to the other helps you remember 幅 means 'width.'

sentence.japanese: この ドア は 幅 が 広いです。
sentence.reading: この ドア は はば が ひろいです。
sentence.romaji: kono doa wa haba ga hiroi desu
sentence.english: This door is wide.

fact: The kanji 複 is common in education and offices. Words like 複雑 (complicated) and 複写 (copying) appear frequently in books, forms, and workplace documents.
memory: Imagine trying to untangle several intertwined ropes. Their complicated arrangement helps you remember 複 means 'complex.'

sentence.japanese: この 問題 は とても 複雑です。
sentence.reading: この もんだい は とても ふくざつです。
sentence.romaji: kono mondai wa totemo fukuzatsu desu
sentence.english: This problem is very complicated.

kunyomi: わ.く, わ.かす, わ.かる
fact: Japanese electric kettles often have a 沸騰 (boiling) indicator that lights up when the water reaches 100°C. This kanji frequently appears on kitchen appliances.
memory: Imagine a kettle bubbling loudly as steam rises from boiling water. Those bubbles help you remember 沸 means 'to boil.'

sentence.japanese: お湯 が 沸いたら 教えてください。
sentence.reading: おゆ が わいたら おしえてください。
sentence.romaji: oyu ga waitara oshiete kudasai
sentence.english: Please tell me when the water boils.

fact: About two-thirds of Japanese people participate in Buddhist traditions during funerals and memorial ceremonies. The kanji 仏 is also used as the abbreviation for France (仏) in newspapers.
memory: Imagine a peaceful Buddha statue sitting calmly inside a quiet temple. That serene figure helps you remember 仏 means 'Buddha.'

sentence.japanese: 奈良 の 大仏 は とても 有名です。
sentence.reading: なら の だいぶつ は とても ゆうめいです。
sentence.romaji: Nara no Daibutsu wa totemo yuumei desu
sentence.english: The Great Buddha of Nara is very famous.

fact: Japan is famous for its tempura and ramen, both of which rely on wheat flour (小麦粉). During spring, 花粉 (pollen) also becomes a major topic because of seasonal allergies.
memory: Imagine dropping a bag of flour onto the floor and watching white powder scatter everywhere. That fine powder helps you remember 粉 means 'powder.'

sentence.japanese: この ケーキ は 小麦粉 を 使います。
sentence.reading: この ケーキ は こむぎこ を つかいます。
sentence.romaji: kono keeki wa komugiko o tsukaimasu
sentence.english: This cake uses wheat flour.

fact: The famous samurai of Japan eventually gave way to a modern army. Today, the kanji 兵 appears in words related to soldiers, military forces, and strategy.
memory: Imagine a soldier standing guard in armor with a spear. That warrior helps you remember 兵 means 'soldier.'

sentence.japanese: 兵士 たち が 訓練 を しています。
sentence.reading: へいし たち が くんれん を しています。
sentence.romaji: heishi tachi ga kunren o shiteimasu
sentence.english: The soldiers are training.

kunyomi: なら.ぶ, なら.べる, なみ
fact: Japanese people are famous for forming orderly queues. Whether boarding trains or buying popular products, people naturally 並ぶ without being told.
memory: Imagine people standing neatly in a straight line waiting for a train. That orderly queue helps you remember 並 means 'to line up.'

sentence.japanese: 店 の 前 に 人 が 並んでいます。
sentence.reading: みせ の まえ に ひと が ならんでいます。
sentence.romaji: mise no mae ni hito ga narandeimasu
sentence.english: People are lined up in front of the store.

fact: The kanji 片 appears in many everyday words, such as 片方 (one side), 片付ける (to tidy up), and 片道 (one-way). It often refers to one side or a single part of something.
memory: Imagine breaking a cookie in half and holding only one piece. That single piece helps you remember 片 means 'one side' or 'fragment.'

sentence.japanese: 部屋 を 片付けました。
sentence.reading: へや を かたづけました。
sentence.romaji: heya o katazukemashita
sentence.english: I cleaned up the room.

fact: Books, TV dramas, and manga are often divided into 編 ('parts' or 'volumes'). The kanji is also used in 編集 (editing), an essential part of publishing and filmmaking.
memory: Imagine an editor carefully arranging chapters of a book before publication. That process of putting things together helps you remember 編 means 'to compile' or 'edit.'

sentence.japanese: 彼 は 雑誌 の 編集者 です。
sentence.reading: かれ は ざっし の へんしゅうしゃ です。
sentence.romaji: kare wa zasshi no henshuusha desu
sentence.english: He is a magazine editor.

fact: The kanji 辺 appears in many place names and directions. Words like 周辺 (surroundings) and 海辺 (seaside) are commonly used in travel guides and everyday conversation.
memory: Imagine standing in the middle of a park and looking all around you. Everything surrounding you is your 'vicinity,' helping you remember 辺 means 'area' or 'around.'

sentence.japanese: この辺 に コンビニ は ありますか。
sentence.reading: このへん に コンビニ は ありますか。
sentence.romaji: kono hen ni konbini wa arimasu ka
sentence.english: Is there a convenience store around here?

fact: The kanji 補 appears frequently in healthcare, education, and business. Words like 補助 (assistance) and 補足 (supplementary explanation) are very common in formal Japanese.
memory: Imagine helping a friend carry a heavy box because they cannot do it alone. Supporting someone helps you remember 補 means 'to assist' or 'supplement.'

sentence.japanese: 先生 が 説明 を 補足しました。
sentence.reading: せんせい が せつめい を ほそくしました。
sentence.romaji: sensei ga setsumei o hosoku shimashita
sentence.english: The teacher added a supplementary explanation.

fact: Job advertisements, volunteer programs, and contests throughout Japan often use the word 募集中 ('Now Recruiting'). This kanji is commonly seen on posters and websites.
memory: Imagine a company putting up a big sign saying 'We're Hiring!' Gathering new people helps you remember 募 means 'to recruit.'

sentence.japanese: 会社 は 新しい 社員 を 募集しています。
sentence.reading: かいしゃ は あたらしい しゃいん を ぼしゅうしています。
sentence.romaji: kaisha wa atarashii shain o boshuu shiteimasu
sentence.english: The company is recruiting new employees.

fact: Gift wrapping is an important part of Japanese culture. Beautifully wrapped presents are common, and the kanji 包 appears in words like 包装 (packaging).
memory: Imagine carefully wrapping a birthday present with colorful paper and ribbon. That wrapped gift helps you remember 包 means 'to wrap.'

sentence.japanese: プレゼント を きれいに 包みました。
sentence.reading: プレゼント を きれいに つつみました。
sentence.romaji: purezento o kirei ni tsutsumimashita
sentence.english: I wrapped the present nicely.

fact: Japan's National Treasures are officially designated as 国宝 (National Treasures). These include famous temples, paintings, swords, and historical artifacts preserved for future generations.
memory: Imagine opening an old treasure chest filled with sparkling gold coins and jewels. That priceless treasure helps you remember 宝 means 'treasure.'

sentence.japanese: 京都 に は 多く の 国宝 が あります。
sentence.reading: きょうと に は おおく の こくほう が あります。
sentence.romaji: Kyouto ni wa ooku no kokuhou ga arimasu
sentence.english: Kyoto has many National Treasures.

fact: Japan celebrates a good harvest every autumn with festivals that give thanks for 豊作 (abundant crops). Rice has long symbolized prosperity and abundance in Japanese culture.
memory: Imagine a rice field so full that the golden stalks bend under their own weight. That overflowing harvest helps you remember 豊 means 'abundant' or 'rich.'

sentence.japanese: この 地域 は 自然 が 豊かです。
sentence.reading: この ちいき は しぜん が ゆたかです。
sentence.romaji: kono chiiki wa shizen ga yutaka desu
sentence.english: This area has abundant nature.

kunyomi: あば.れる, あば.く, あば.く
fact: The kanji 暴 appears in many news reports, such as 暴風 (violent wind) and 暴力 (violence). It often describes something intense or out of control.
memory: Imagine a powerful storm violently shaking trees and buildings. That uncontrolled force helps you remember 暴 means 'violent.'

sentence.japanese: 台風 が 暴風 を もたらしました。
sentence.reading: たいふう が ぼうふう を もたらしました。
sentence.romaji: taifuu ga boufuu o motarashimashita
sentence.english: The typhoon brought violent winds.

fact: The kanji 棒 appears in many everyday objects, including 鉄棒 (horizontal bar), used in Japanese school playgrounds, and 棒読み, meaning reading in a flat, emotionless voice.
memory: Imagine holding a long wooden stick while hiking. That straight rod helps you remember 棒 means 'stick.'

sentence.japanese: 子ども が 鉄棒 で 遊んでいます。
sentence.reading: こども が てつぼう で あそんでいます。
sentence.romaji: kodomo ga tetsubou de asondeimasu
sentence.english: The children are playing on the horizontal bar.

fact: Japan is one of the world's largest trading nations. The word 貿易 (international trade) appears frequently in economics, business, and world news.
memory: Imagine cargo ships carrying goods between countries across the ocean. That international exchange helps you remember 貿 means 'trade.'

sentence.japanese: 日本 は 多く の 国 と 貿易 を しています。
sentence.reading: にほん は おおく の くに と ぼうえき を しています。
sentence.romaji: nihon wa ooku no kuni to boueki o shiteimasu
sentence.english: Japan trades with many countries.

fact: Japan has one of the world's most advanced healthcare systems. The kanji 療 appears in common medical words such as 治療 (treatment) and 医療 (medical care).
memory: Imagine a doctor carefully treating a patient until they recover. That medical care helps you remember 療 means 'treatment.'

sentence.japanese: 病院 で 治療 を 受けました。
sentence.reading: びょういん で ちりょう を うけました。
sentence.romaji: byouin de chiryou o ukemashita
sentence.english: I received medical treatment at the hospital.

fact: Japanese recipes carefully distinguish between 量 (amount) and 重さ (weight). Cooking guides often use 適量 (tekiryou), meaning 'an appropriate amount,' instead of giving an exact measurement.
memory: Imagine a chef carefully measuring the right amount of flour before baking. Measuring the correct quantity helps you remember 量 means 'amount.'

sentence.japanese: 水 を 適量 入れてください。
sentence.reading: みず を てきりょう いれてください。
sentence.romaji: mizu o tekiryou irete kudasai
sentence.english: Please add an appropriate amount of water.

fact: The kanji 領 is frequently used in government, technology, and education. Words like 領土 (territory) and 領収書 (receipt) are among the most common compounds encountered in daily life.
memory: Imagine a king standing at the border of his kingdom, protecting his territory. That land under his control helps you remember 領 means 'territory.'

sentence.japanese: レジ で 領収書 を もらいました。
sentence.reading: レジ で りょうしゅうしょ を もらいました。
sentence.romaji: reji de ryoushuusho o moraimashita
sentence.english: I received a receipt at the register.

fact: Japan has a national holiday called みどりの日 (Greenery Day), which celebrates nature and encourages people to appreciate the environment.
memory: Imagine walking through a forest filled with vibrant green trees and fresh leaves. That lush scenery helps you remember 緑 means 'green.'

sentence.japanese: 緑茶 を 毎日 飲みます。
sentence.reading: りょくちゃ を まいにち のみます。
sentence.romaji: ryokucha o mainichi nomimasu
sentence.english: I drink green tea every day.

fact: The Olympic symbol consists of five interlocking rings. In Japanese, it is called 五輪 (gorin), and the Olympics are often simply referred to as 五輪 in newspapers.
memory: Imagine a bicycle wheel spinning smoothly in a perfect circle. That round wheel helps you remember 輪 means 'wheel' or 'ring.'

sentence.japanese: 彼女 は 指輪 を しています。
sentence.reading: かのじょ は ゆびわ を しています。
sentence.romaji: kanojo wa yubiwa o shiteimasu
sentence.english: She is wearing a ring.

fact: Japanese dramas and anime often describe emotional scenes as 涙なしでは見られない ('impossible to watch without tears'), making 涙 one of the most expressive emotion-related kanji.
memory: Imagine tears slowly rolling down someone's face after hearing touching news. Those falling tears help you remember 涙 means 'tears.'

sentence.japanese: 感動して 涙 が 出ました。
sentence.reading: かんどうして なみだ が でました。
sentence.romaji: kandou shite namida ga demashita
sentence.english: I was so moved that tears came to my eyes.

fact: Japan's current era name, 令和 (Reiwa), begins with the kanji 令. It became one of the most recognized kanji in the country when the Reiwa era started in 2019.
memory: Imagine an emperor issuing an official command that everyone must follow. That royal order helps you remember 令 means 'command' or 'decree.'

sentence.japanese: 上司 の 命令 に 従いました。
sentence.reading: じょうし の めいれい に したがいました。
sentence.romaji: joushi no meirei ni shitagaimashita
sentence.english: I obeyed my supervisor's order.

fact: Although Japanese usually writes the number 0 as 「0」 or ゼロ, the kanji 零 is commonly used in formal writing, mathematics, weather reports, and terms like 零下 ('below zero').
memory: Imagine a thermometer dropping to exactly zero degrees. That point on the scale helps you remember 零 means 'zero.'

sentence.japanese: 今朝 は 気温 が 零下 に なりました。
sentence.reading: けさ は きおん が れいか に なりました。
sentence.romaji: kesa wa kion ga reika ni narimashita
sentence.english: The temperature fell below zero this morning.

fact: The kanji 齢 is mostly found in formal words such as 年齢 (age) and 高齢者 (elderly person). Everyday conversation usually uses 年 instead.
memory: Imagine counting the candles on a birthday cake to determine someone's age. Those birthdays help you remember 齢 means 'age.'

sentence.japanese: 年齢 を 教えてください。
sentence.reading: ねんれい を おしえてください。
sentence.romaji: nenrei o oshiete kudasai
sentence.english: Please tell me your age.

fact: The kanji 歴 appears in many important words such as 履歴書 (resume), 歴史 (history), and 経歴 (career history). It's one of the most useful kanji for work and study.
memory: Imagine flipping through a timeline filled with important events from the past. That long record helps you remember 歴 means 'history.'

sentence.japanese: 履歴書 を 提出しました。
sentence.reading: りれきしょ を ていしゅつしました。
sentence.romaji: rirekisho o teishutsu shimashita
sentence.english: I submitted my resume.

kunyomi: こ.い, こい.する, こ.う
fact: Japanese distinguishes between 恋 (romantic love or longing) and 愛 (deep love or affection). This difference appears frequently in songs, anime, novels, and poetry.
memory: Imagine someone gazing at the moon while thinking about the person they love. That feeling of longing helps you remember 恋 means 'romantic love.'

sentence.japanese: 彼 は 初恋 の 思い出 を 話しました。
sentence.reading: かれ は はつこい の おもいで を はなしました。
sentence.romaji: kare wa hatsukoi no omoide o hanashimashita
sentence.english: He talked about his first love.

fact: The word 練習 (renshū, practice) is one of the first words Japanese learners hear. In Japan, musicians, athletes, and students all emphasize daily 練習 as the key to improvement.
memory: Imagine a blacksmith heating and folding steel over and over to make a perfect sword. Repeated refining helps you remember 練 means 'to practice' or 'to refine.'

words.meaning: To knead, To polish an idea

sentence.japanese: 毎日 日本語 を 練習しています。
sentence.reading: まいにち にほんご を れんしゅうしています。
sentence.romaji: mainichi nihongo o renshuu shiteimasu
sentence.english: I practice Japanese every day.

fact: The kanji 録 appears in modern technology every day. Words like 録音 (audio recording) and 録画 (video recording) are common on smartphones, TVs, and computers.
memory: Imagine pressing the red RECORD button on a camera. Capturing sound and video helps you remember 録 means 'record.'

sentence.japanese: 会議 を 録音しました。
sentence.reading: かいぎ を ろくおんしました。
sentence.romaji: kaigi o rokuon shimashita
sentence.english: I recorded the meeting.

fact: Tokyo Bay (東京湾) is one of Japan's most important coastal regions, home to major ports, airports, and attractions such as Tokyo Disney Resort.
memory: Imagine a calm curved coastline protecting boats from the open sea. That sheltered body of water helps you remember 湾 means 'bay.'

sentence.japanese: 船 が 東京湾 を 出発しました。
sentence.reading: ふね が とうきょうわん を しゅっぱつしました。
sentence.romaji: fune ga Toukyouwan o shuppatsu shimashita
sentence.english: The ship departed from Tokyo Bay.

fact: In Japanese, 腕 doesn't only mean 'arm.' Expressions like 腕がいい literally mean 'good arm,' but actually describe someone who is highly skilled at their job.
memory: Imagine a master craftsman confidently using his strong arms to create beautiful furniture. His skilled arms help you remember 腕 means both 'arm' and 'skill.'

sentence.japanese: 彼 は 料理 の 腕 が いいです。
sentence.reading: かれ は りょうり の うで が いいです。
sentence.romaji: kare wa ryouri no ude ga ii desu
sentence.english: He is good at cooking.

fact: Japan frequently prepares for earthquakes, typhoons, and tsunamis. The word 防災 (disaster prevention) is seen on evacuation maps, schools, and public facilities throughout the country.
memory: Imagine a strong castle wall protecting a town from danger. That defense helps you remember 防 means 'to protect' or 'to prevent.'

sentence.japanese: 病気 を 防ぐ ために 手 を 洗います。
sentence.reading: びょうき を ふせぐ ために て を あらいます。
sentence.romaji: byouki o fusegu tame ni te o araimasu
sentence.english: I wash my hands to prevent illness.

fact: Japanese craftspeople often spend years polishing (磨く) their skills. The phrase 腕を磨く literally means 'to polish one's arm,' but actually means 'to improve one's skills.'
memory: Imagine polishing a dull sword until it shines like a mirror. That shining blade helps you remember 磨 means 'to polish.'

sentence.japanese: 毎日 日本語 の 腕 を 磨いています。
sentence.reading: まいにち にほんご の うで を みがいています。
sentence.romaji: mainichi nihongo no ude o migaiteimasu
sentence.english: I polish my Japanese skills every day.

kunyomi: う.める, う.まる, うず.める
fact: Much of Tokyo Bay has been expanded using 埋め立て (land reclamation), where land is created by filling in shallow coastal waters.
memory: Imagine burying a treasure chest underground until it completely disappears. Covering it with earth helps you remember 埋 means 'to bury.'

sentence.japanese: 穴 を 土 で 埋めました。
sentence.reading: あな を つち で うめました。
sentence.romaji: ana o tsuchi de umemashita
sentence.english: I filled the hole with soil.

fact: Japanese uses 枚 as the counter for flat objects such as paper, tickets, photos, and plates. It's one of the most frequently used counters in everyday conversation.
memory: Imagine counting five sheets of paper one by one. Those flat sheets help you remember 枚 is the counter for flat objects.

sentence.japanese: 紙 を 一枚 ください。
sentence.reading: かみ を いちまい ください。
sentence.romaji: kami o ichimai kudasai
sentence.english: Please give me one sheet of paper.

fact: Japan exports millions of vehicles every year. The kanji 輸 appears in words like 輸出 (export) and 輸入 (import), making it common in business and economics.
memory: Imagine cargo ships carrying containers across the ocean between countries. That movement of goods helps you remember 輸 means 'transport.'

sentence.japanese: 日本 は 車 を 多く 輸出しています。
sentence.reading: にほん は くるま を おおく ゆしゅつしています。
sentence.romaji: nihon wa kuruma o ooku yushutsu shiteimasu
sentence.english: Japan exports many cars.

fact: The spirit of courage is celebrated in many Japanese legends and samurai stories. The word 勇気 (courage) is one of the most common motivational words in Japanese.
