memory: Imagine a brave samurai stepping forward to protect others despite the danger. That fearless act helps you remember 勇 means 'courage.'

sentence.japanese: 勇気 を 出して 話しかけました。
sentence.reading: ゆうき を だして はなしかけました。
sentence.romaji: yuuki o dashite hanashikakemashita
sentence.english: I gathered my courage and started talking.

fact: Japan's postal service is famous for its speed and reliability. The red 〒 postal mark is recognized nationwide, and the kanji 郵 appears in many words related to mail and delivery.
memory: Imagine dropping a letter into Japan's famous red postbox. That journey through the postal system helps you remember 郵 means 'mail.'

sentence.japanese: 郵便局 で 手紙 を 出しました。
sentence.reading: ゆうびんきょく で てがみ を だしました。
sentence.romaji: yuubinkyoku de tegami o dashimashita
sentence.english: I mailed a letter at the post office.

kunyomi: あず.ける, あず.かる
fact: Banks, hotels, and train stations in Japan often use 預かり所 (cloakrooms or baggage storage), where travelers can safely leave their belongings.
memory: Imagine handing your suitcase to a hotel receptionist for safekeeping. Entrusting your belongings helps you remember 預 means 'to deposit' or 'to entrust.'

sentence.japanese: 銀行 に お金 を 預けています。
sentence.reading: ぎんこう に おかね を あずけています。
sentence.romaji: ginkou ni okane o azuketeimasu
sentence.english: I keep my money in the bank.

fact: The kanji 幼 appears in words related to children, such as 幼稚園 (kindergarten). Japanese kindergartens place a strong emphasis on social development and independence.
memory: Imagine a small child taking their first steps into kindergarten while holding a parent's hand. That young child helps you remember 幼 means 'young.'

sentence.japanese: 彼女 は 幼い 頃 から ピアノ を 習っています。
sentence.reading: かのじょ は おさない ころ から ピアノ を ならっています。
sentence.romaji: kanojo wa osanai koro kara piano o naratteimasu
sentence.english: She has studied piano since childhood.

kunyomi: と.ける, と.かす, と.く
fact: Science classes in Japan frequently use the word 溶液 (solution) when learning about chemistry. The kanji 溶 is used whenever something melts or dissolves into a liquid.
memory: Imagine a sugar cube slowly disappearing into a cup of hot tea. Watching it dissolve helps you remember 溶 means 'to melt' or 'to dissolve.'

sentence.japanese: 砂糖 を お湯 に 溶かしました。
sentence.reading: さとう を おゆ に とかしました。
sentence.romaji: satou o oyu ni tokashimashita
sentence.english: I dissolved sugar in hot water.

fact: During Japan's Obon festival, people gather to perform 盆踊り (Bon Odori), a traditional folk dance that has been celebrated for centuries across the country.
memory: Imagine a circle of people dancing together under colorful lanterns at a summer festival. Their joyful movements help you remember 踊 means 'to dance.'

sentence.japanese: 祭り で みんな が 踊っています。
sentence.reading: まつり で みんな が おどっています。
sentence.romaji: matsuri de minna ga odotteimasu
sentence.english: Everyone is dancing at the festival.

fact: Bathing is an important part of Japanese culture. Whether visiting an onsen (hot spring) or a sentō (public bath), people often use the word 入浴 (taking a bath).
memory: Imagine relaxing in a steaming Japanese hot spring while warm water surrounds your body. That soothing bath helps you remember 浴 means 'to bathe.'

sentence.japanese: 仕事 の あとで お風呂 に 入浴しました。
sentence.reading: しごと の あとで おふろ に にゅうよくしました。
sentence.romaji: shigoto no ato de ofuro ni nyuuyoku shimashita
sentence.english: I took a bath after work.

fact: Japanese newspapers and official documents frequently use 翌 to refer to the following day, month, or year. It sounds more formal than simply saying 次の日.
memory: Imagine flipping a calendar page to the very next day. That following page helps you remember 翌 means 'the next' or 'following.'

sentence.japanese: 翌日 は 雨 が 降りました。
sentence.reading: よくじつ は あめ が ふりました。
sentence.romaji: yokujitsu wa ame ga furimashita
sentence.english: It rained the following day.

kunyomi: から.む, から.まる, から.める
fact: The word 連絡 (contact) is one of the most common business expressions in Japanese. Students and office workers hear 『後で連絡します』 ('I'll contact you later') almost every day.
memory: Imagine two telephone wires twisting together while connecting two people. Those tangled connections help you remember 絡 means 'to connect' or 'to become tangled.'

sentence.japanese: 後で 電話 で 連絡します。
sentence.reading: あとで でんわ で れんらくします。
sentence.romaji: ato de denwa de renraku shimasu
sentence.english: I'll contact you by phone later.

fact: One of Japan's most famous historical periods is the 戦国時代 ('Warring States Period'), a time of political turmoil often described with the kanji 乱, as in 応仁の乱 (Ōnin War).
memory: Imagine papers flying everywhere after a strong gust of wind. That messy disorder helps you remember 乱 means 'chaos.'

sentence.japanese: 交通 が 混乱しています。
sentence.reading: こうつう が こんらんしています。
sentence.romaji: koutsuu ga konran shiteimasu
sentence.english: Traffic is in confusion.

fact: Japan consumes one of the highest numbers of eggs per person in the world. Fresh eggs are commonly eaten raw over rice in the famous dish 卵かけご飯 (Tamago Kake Gohan).
memory: Imagine cracking a fresh egg into a bowl and seeing the bright yellow yolk appear. That egg helps you remember 卵 means 'egg.'

sentence.japanese: 朝ご飯 に 卵焼き を 食べました。
sentence.reading: あさごはん に たまごやき を たべました。
sentence.romaji: asagohan ni tamagoyaki o tabemashita
sentence.english: I ate a Japanese omelet for breakfast.

fact: Many Japanese maps and tickets use 表 (front) and 裏 (back) to indicate which side to read. The expression 裏口 (back entrance) is also common in everyday life.
memory: Imagine flipping over a playing card to see its hidden back side. That opposite side helps you remember 裏 means 'back' or 'reverse.'

sentence.japanese: 紙 の 裏 に 名前 を 書いてください。
sentence.reading: かみ の うら に なまえ を かいてください。
sentence.romaji: kami no ura ni namae o kaite kudasai
sentence.english: Please write your name on the back of the paper.

fact: Japan is an island nation, so the contrast between 海 (sea) and 陸 (land) is especially important. The kanji appears in words like 大陸 (continent) and 上陸 (landing ashore).
memory: Imagine a ship finally reaching solid land after days at sea. That safe shoreline helps you remember 陸 means 'land.'

sentence.japanese: 飛行機 が 無事 に 着陸しました。
sentence.reading: ひこうき が ぶじ に ちゃくりくしました。
sentence.romaji: hikouki ga buji ni chakuriku shimashita
sentence.english: The airplane landed safely.

fact: The kanji 律 appears in words related to laws, discipline, and music. The word 法律 (law) is one of the most common compounds using this kanji.
memory: Imagine a judge reading a law book while a metronome keeps perfect rhythm beside him. Rules and rhythm help you remember 律 means 'law' or 'regulation.'

sentence.japanese: 学生 は 学校 の 規律 を 守ります。
sentence.reading: がくせい は がっこう の きりつ を まもります。
sentence.romaji: gakusei wa gakkou no kiritsu o mamorimasu
sentence.english: Students follow the school's rules.

fact: Japanese often shortens long words into abbreviations. For example, パーソナルコンピューター became パソコン. The kanji 略 appears in words such as 省略 (omission) and 略語 (abbreviation).
memory: Imagine taking a long paragraph and crossing out unnecessary words until only the important points remain. That shortened version helps you remember 略 means 'abbreviation.'

sentence.japanese: 説明 は 一部 省略します。
sentence.reading: せつめい は いちぶ しょうりゃくします。
sentence.romaji: setsumei wa ichibu shouryaku shimasu
sentence.english: I will omit part of the explanation.

fact: Rice is central to Japanese culture, and 粒 is the counter for small grains and droplets. The word 米粒 literally means 'a grain of rice.'
memory: Imagine holding a single grain of rice between your fingers. That tiny grain helps you remember 粒 means 'grain' or 'particle.'

sentence.japanese: 雨粒 が 窓 に 当たっています。
sentence.reading: あまつぶ が まど に あたっています。
sentence.romaji: amatsubu ga mado ni atatteimasu
sentence.english: Raindrops are hitting the window.

fact: The kanji 了 is commonly seen in formal notices and computer interfaces. Words like 完了 (completion) appear whenever a download, payment, or application has successfully finished.
memory: Imagine seeing a big green 'Completed' checkmark after finishing an online task. That completed status helps you remember 了 means 'finish.'

sentence.japanese: 作業 が 完了しました。
sentence.reading: さぎょう が かんりょうしました。
sentence.romaji: sagyou ga kanryou shimashita
sentence.english: The work has been completed.

kunyomi: すず.しい, すず.む, すず.やか
fact: During Japan's hot summers, people seek 涼 (coolness) by visiting rivers, mountains, and enjoying wind chimes (風鈴), which are believed to create a refreshing feeling.
memory: Imagine standing beside a cool mountain stream on a hot summer day. That refreshing breeze helps you remember 涼 means 'cool.'

words.reading: せいりょういんりょうすい

sentence.japanese: 今日は とても 涼しいです。
sentence.reading: きょう は とても すずしいです。
sentence.romaji: kyou wa totemo suzushii desu
sentence.english: It's very cool today.

fact: The kanji 扱 is extremely common in shops and offices. Signs saying 『取扱中』 indicate that a store carries or deals with a particular product.
memory: Imagine carefully handling a fragile glass vase with both hands. Treating it gently helps you remember 扱 means 'to handle.'

words.reading: とりあつかいせつめいしょ
とりあつかいちゅうい

sentence.japanese: この 機械 は 丁寧 に 扱ってください。
sentence.reading: この きかい は ていねい に あつかってください。
sentence.romaji: kono kikai wa teinei ni atsukatte kudasai
sentence.english: Please handle this machine carefully.

fact: The kanji 案 is widely used in offices and meetings. Words like 提案 (proposal) and 案内 (guidance) appear frequently in business and travel situations.
memory: Imagine someone presenting a detailed plan on a whiteboard during a meeting. That proposal helps you remember 案 means 'plan' or 'idea.'

sentence.japanese: 新しい 提案 を 考えました。
sentence.reading: あたらしい ていあん を かんがえました。
sentence.romaji: atarashii teian o kangaemashita
sentence.english: I came up with a new proposal.

kunyomi: ため, な.る, な.す
fact: Although uncommon by itself, 為 appears in many important expressions such as 為替 (foreign exchange) and 人の為 (for the sake of others). In modern Japanese, it is often seen in formal writing.
memory: Imagine working hard for the sake of your family. Acting for someone else's benefit helps you remember 為 means 'for the sake of.'

sentence.japanese: 家族 の 為 に 一生懸命 働いています。
sentence.reading: かぞく の ため に いっしょうけんめい はたらいています。
sentence.romaji: kazoku no tame ni isshoukenmei hataraiteimasu
sentence.english: I work hard for the sake of my family.

kunyomi: こと.なる, こと.にする
fact: The kanji 異 appears in many academic and scientific words, such as 異文化 (different culture) and 異常 (abnormal). It is commonly used to compare things that are different from one another.
memory: Imagine one red apple sitting among many green apples. Being the only one that's different helps you remember 異 means 'different' or 'unusual.'

sentence.japanese: 二人 の 意見 は 異なります。
sentence.reading: ふたり の いけん は ことなります。
sentence.romaji: futari no iken wa kotonarimasu
sentence.english: The two people's opinions are different.

fact: Japan has many 'hidden gems' known as 隠れた名所. The kanji 隠 is often used for places, talents, or truths that remain hidden from most people.
memory: Imagine playing hide-and-seek and disappearing behind a large tree. Being hidden from view helps you remember 隠 means 'to hide.'

sentence.japanese: 子ども は テーブル の 下 に 隠れました。
sentence.reading: こども は テーブル の した に かくれました。
sentence.romaji: kodomo wa teeburu no shita ni kakuremashita
sentence.english: The child hid under the table.

fact: Traditional Japanese shadow plays and modern photography both rely on the concept of 影. The kanji also appears metaphorically to describe someone's influence or presence.
memory: Imagine your shadow following you everywhere under the bright sun. That dark outline helps you remember 影 means 'shadow.'

sentence.japanese: 木 の 影 で 休みました。
sentence.reading: き の かげ で やすみました。
sentence.romaji: ki no kage de yasumimashita
sentence.english: I rested in the shade of a tree.

fact: After natural disasters, Japan often organizes 支援活動 (support activities) to help affected communities. The kanji 援 is widely used in government and charity work.
memory: Imagine reaching out your hand to help someone climb up a steep hill. That helping hand reminds you that 援 means 'to support.'

sentence.japanese: みんな が 私 を 応援してくれました。
sentence.reading: みんな が わたし を おうえんしてくれました。
sentence.romaji: minna ga watashi o ouen shite kuremashita
sentence.english: Everyone cheered me on.

fact: The kanji 応 appears in many everyday words such as 応募 (application), 応答 (response), and 対応 (dealing with). It is one of the most useful business kanji in Japanese.
memory: Imagine your phone ringing and you immediately answer the call. Responding right away helps you remember 応 means 'to respond.'

sentence.japanese: 質問 に すぐ 応答しました。
sentence.reading: しつもん に すぐ おうとうしました。
sentence.romaji: shitsumon ni sugu outou shimashita
sentence.english: I responded to the question immediately.

fact: The concept of 恩 (on) has been important in Japanese culture for centuries. It refers to a kindness or favor that creates a lifelong sense of gratitude and obligation to repay it.
memory: Imagine someone helping you during your hardest moment. You never forget their kindness, helping you remember 恩 means 'favor' or 'kindness.'

sentence.japanese: 先生 の 恩 を 一生 忘れません。
sentence.reading: せんせい の おん を いっしょう わすれません。
sentence.romaji: sensei no on o isshou wasuremasen
sentence.english: I will never forget my teacher's kindness.

fact: Weather forecasts in Japan often describe calm seas as 穏やかな海. The kanji is also used to describe gentle personalities and peaceful situations.
memory: Imagine a lake without a single ripple reflecting the blue sky. That peaceful scene helps you remember 穏 means 'calm.'

sentence.japanese: 今日は 海 が 穏やかです。
sentence.reading: きょう は うみ が おだやかです。
sentence.romaji: kyou wa umi ga odayaka desu
sentence.english: The sea is calm today.

fact: The verb 稼ぐ is widely used in business, sports, and online media. It can mean earning money, scoring points, or generating website traffic.
memory: Imagine working hard every day until your wallet becomes full. That hard work helps you remember 稼 means 'to earn.'

words.reading: がいか を かせぐ

sentence.japanese: 彼 は 毎日 一生懸命 働いて お金 を 稼いでいます。
sentence.reading: かれ は まいにち いっしょうけんめい はたらいて おかね を かせいでいます。
sentence.romaji: kare wa mainichi isshoukenmei hataraite okane o kaseideimasu
sentence.english: He works hard every day to earn money.

kunyomi: あや.しい, あや.しむ, あや.しむ
fact: Japan has a rich tradition of 妖怪 (yōkai), mysterious supernatural creatures found in folklore, anime, and games. The kanji 怪 often conveys something eerie or suspicious.
memory: Imagine seeing a mysterious shadow moving in an abandoned house at night. That strange sight helps you remember 怪 means 'mysterious.'

sentence.japanese: その 男 は 少し 怪しかったです。
sentence.reading: その おとこ は すこし あやしかったです。
sentence.romaji: sono otoko wa sukoshi ayashikatta desu
sentence.english: That man looked a little suspicious.

kunyomi: く.やむ, くや.しい, く.いる
fact: The phrase 悔いのない人生 ('a life without regrets') is a popular expression in Japanese books, speeches, and motivational quotes.
memory: Imagine looking back at a missed opportunity and wishing you had acted differently. That feeling helps you remember 悔 means 'regret.'

sentence.japanese: 後で 後悔 したくありません。
sentence.reading: あとで こうかい したくありません。
sentence.romaji: ato de koukai shitaku arimasen
sentence.english: I don't want to regret it later.

kunyomi: なつ.かしい, なつ.かしむ, ふところ, いだ.く, いだ.かれる
fact: The adjective 懐かしい is one of the most uniquely Japanese emotional expressions. It describes the warm feeling of remembering a cherished memory, not simply 'missing' something.
memory: Imagine opening an old photo album and smiling as happy memories come rushing back. That warm nostalgic feeling helps you remember 懐 means 'nostalgic.'

sentence.japanese: この 歌 を 聞く と 懐かしい 気持ち に なります。
sentence.reading: この うた を きく と なつかしい きもち に なります。
sentence.romaji: kono uta o kiku to natsukashii kimochi ni narimasu
sentence.english: Hearing this song makes me feel nostalgic.

fact: The kanji 較 almost always appears in compound words such as 比較 (comparison). It is one of the most useful kanji in academic writing and business reports.
memory: Imagine placing two smartphones side by side to compare their features. Looking at both together helps you remember 較 means 'compare.'

sentence.japanese: 二つ の 商品 を 比較しました。
sentence.reading: ふたつ の しょうひん を ひかくしました。
sentence.romaji: futatsu no shouhin o hikaku shimashita
sentence.english: I compared the two products.

fact: During winter, weather reports often warn about 路面が滑りやすい ('roads are slippery'). The kanji 滑 is used for both physical slipperiness and smooth movement.
memory: Imagine stepping onto an icy road and suddenly slipping forward. That slippery surface helps you remember 滑 means 'to slip.'

sentence.japanese: 雨 の 日 は 道 が 滑りやすいです。
sentence.reading: あめ の ひ は みち が すべりやすいです。
sentence.romaji: ame no hi wa michi ga suberiyasui desu
sentence.english: The road is slippery on rainy days.

fact: The Japanese stock market uses the word 株 extensively. Companies are called 株式会社 (joint-stock company), one of the first business terms learners encounter.
memory: Imagine buying one share of your favorite company on the stock market. That share certificate helps you remember 株 means 'stock.'

sentence.japanese: 彼 は 株 を 買いました。
sentence.reading: かれ は かぶ を かいました。
sentence.romaji: kare wa kabu o kaimashita
sentence.english: He bought stocks.

fact: The kanji 勧 appears in everyday words such as 勧める (to recommend) and 勧誘 (solicitation). Restaurants, shops, and friends often use お勧め to recommend popular items.
memory: Imagine a friend enthusiastically recommending their favorite restaurant. Their encouragement helps you remember 勧 means 'to recommend.'

sentence.japanese: 友達 が この 本 を 勧めてくれました。
sentence.reading: ともだち が この ほん を すすめてくれました。
sentence.romaji: tomodachi ga kono hon o susumete kuremashita
sentence.english: My friend recommended this book to me.

fact: The expression 勘がいい ('to have good intuition') is commonly used in Japanese. Detectives in mystery novels and experienced craftsmen are often praised for their sharp 勘.
memory: Imagine solving a difficult puzzle without any clues because your instincts guide you. That gut feeling helps you remember 勘 means 'intuition.'

sentence.japanese: 彼 は 勘 が 鋭いです。
sentence.reading: かれ は かん が するどいです。
sentence.romaji: kare wa kan ga surudoi desu
sentence.english: He has sharp intuition.

fact: The kanji 価 is used throughout Japan in shopping, economics, and investing. Words like 価格 (price) and 価値 (value) are among the most common business vocabulary.
memory: Imagine looking at a price tag before buying something. That price helps you remember 価 means 'value' or 'price.'

sentence.japanese: この 商品 は 価格 が 高いです。
sentence.reading: この しょうひん は かかく が たかいです。
sentence.romaji: kono shouhin wa kakaku ga takai desu
sentence.english: This product is expensive.

kunyomi: ゆる.い, ゆる.やか, ゆる.む, ゆる.める
fact: Road signs in Japan often display the word 徐行 or ask drivers to speed down on 緩やかなカーブ ('gentle curves'). The kanji 緩 is also used to describe relaxed rules or gradual slopes.
memory: Imagine loosening a tight rope until it hangs freely. That relaxed rope helps you remember 緩 means 'to loosen.'

sentence.japanese: ネクタイ を 少し 緩めました。
sentence.reading: ネクタイ を すこし ゆるめました。
sentence.romaji: nekutai o sukoshi yurumemashita
sentence.english: I loosened my necktie a little.

fact: The adjective 嬉しい is one of the most common words for expressing happiness in Japanese. Unlike 楽しい, which describes fun experiences, 嬉しい expresses joy about good news or kindness.
memory: Imagine receiving unexpected good news and smiling from ear to ear. That joyful feeling helps you remember 嬉 means 'happy.'

sentence.japanese: プレゼント を もらって とても 嬉しかったです。
sentence.reading: プレゼント を もらって とても うれしかったです。
sentence.romaji: purezento o moratte totemo ureshikatta desu
sentence.english: I was very happy to receive the present.

kunyomi: たっと.い, とうと.い, たっと.ぶ, とうと.ぶ
fact: The kanji 貴 is often used in polite business Japanese. Expressions like 貴社 ('your company') and 貴重 ('valuable') are commonly seen in formal letters and emails.
memory: Imagine holding a priceless diamond in your hands. That precious gem helps you remember 貴 means 'valuable' or 'noble.'

sentence.japanese: これは とても 貴重な 資料 です。
sentence.reading: これは とても きちょうな しりょう です。
sentence.romaji: kore wa totemo kichouna shiryou desu
sentence.english: This is very valuable material.

fact: The expression 稀な出来事 ('a rare event') is often used in Japanese news reports to describe unusual natural phenomena or exceptional achievements.
memory: Imagine finding a four-leaf clover among thousands of ordinary ones. That uncommon discovery helps you remember 稀 means 'rare.'

sentence.japanese: こんな こと は とても 稀です。
sentence.reading: こんな こと は とても まれです。
sentence.romaji: konna koto wa totemo mare desu
sentence.english: Something like this is very rare.

kunyomi: かがや.く, かがや.き
fact: The kanji 輝 is commonly found in names because it symbolizes brilliance, hope, and success. It also appears in phrases describing shining stars or bright futures.
memory: Imagine the sun shining so brightly that everything sparkles. That dazzling light helps you remember 輝 means 'to shine.'

words.reading: えいこう に かがやく

sentence.japanese: 星 が 夜空 に 輝いています。
sentence.reading: ほし が よぞら に かがやいています。
sentence.romaji: hoshi ga yozora ni kagayaiteimasu
sentence.english: The stars are shining in the night sky.

fact: Japan is famous for traditional ceremonies such as the tea ceremony (茶道) and coming-of-age celebrations. The kanji 儀 appears in words for formal ceremonies and etiquette.
memory: Imagine guests carefully following every step of a traditional tea ceremony. That formal ritual helps you remember 儀 means 'ceremony.'

sentence.japanese: 卒業式 の 儀式 が 始まりました。
sentence.reading: そつぎょうしき の ぎしき が はじまりました。
sentence.romaji: sotsugyoushiki no gishiki ga hajimarimashita
sentence.english: The graduation ceremony began.

kunyomi: およ.ぶ, およ.び, およ.ぼす
fact: The kanji 及 is frequently used in formal writing to describe effects or influence extending to something, such as 影響が及ぶ ('an effect reaches...').
memory: Imagine stretching your hand just far enough to reach a book on a high shelf. That reaching motion helps you remember 及 means 'to reach.'

sentence.japanese: 台風 が 農業 に 影響 を 及ぼしました。
sentence.reading: たいふう が のうぎょう に えいきょう を およぼしました。
sentence.romaji: taifuu ga nougyou ni eikyou o oyoboshimashita
sentence.english: The typhoon affected agriculture.

fact: The expression 泣き笑い ('crying and laughing at the same time') is commonly used in Japanese to describe overwhelming emotions. Japanese films and anime often describe touching stories as 泣ける ('it makes you cry').
memory: Imagine tears rolling down someone's face after hearing emotional news. Those tears help you remember 泣 means 'to cry.'

sentence.japanese: 映画 を 見て 泣いてしまいました。
sentence.reading: えいが を みて ないてしまいました。
sentence.romaji: eiga o mite naite shimaimashita
sentence.english: I ended up crying after watching the movie.

kunyomi: せま.い, せば.める, せば.まる
fact: Many traditional Japanese streets and alleys are quite narrow. The expression 狭い道 ('narrow road') is commonly seen on road signs and maps.
memory: Imagine trying to squeeze through a very narrow alley where only one person can pass. That tight space helps you remember 狭 means 'narrow.'

sentence.japanese: この 部屋 は 少し 狭いです。
sentence.reading: この へや は すこし せまいです。
sentence.romaji: kono heya wa sukoshi semai desu
sentence.english: This room is a little small.

fact: The kanji 刑 appears in many legal terms, such as 刑事 (criminal detective) and 刑務所 (prison). It is commonly encountered in news reports and crime dramas.
memory: Imagine a judge announcing a criminal's sentence in court. That legal punishment helps you remember 刑 means 'penalty.'

sentence.japanese: その 事件 は 刑事 が 調べています。
sentence.reading: その じけん は けいじ が しらべています。
sentence.romaji: sono jiken wa keiji ga shirabeteimasu
sentence.english: The case is being investigated by detectives.

fact: The kanji 恵 often appears in names because it symbolizes blessings and kindness. The common word 恵み refers to blessings from nature, such as rain or a good harvest.
memory: Imagine gentle rain falling on dry farmland, bringing life to the crops. That blessing from nature helps you remember 恵 means 'blessing.'

sentence.japanese: 自然 の 恵み に 感謝しています。
sentence.reading: しぜん の めぐみ に かんしゃしています。
sentence.romaji: shizen no megumi ni kansha shiteimasu
sentence.english: I am grateful for nature's blessings.

fact: The kanji 激 appears in words describing strong emotions and powerful natural events. 激辛 ('extremely spicy') has even become a popular food challenge in Japan.
memory: Imagine a fierce storm with strong winds and heavy rain. That powerful force helps you remember 激 means 'intense' or 'violent.'

sentence.japanese: 昨日 は 雨 が 激しく 降りました。
sentence.reading: きのう は あめ が はげしく ふりました。
sentence.romaji: kinou wa ame ga hageshiku furimashita
sentence.english: It rained heavily yesterday.

fact: The kanji 健 appears in many everyday words related to health, such as 健康 (health) and 保健 (public health). It's one of the most useful kanji for discussing fitness and well-being in Japanese.
memory: Imagine someone jogging every morning and eating healthy food. Their strong and energetic body helps you remember 健 means 'healthy.'

sentence.japanese: 健康 の ために 毎日 運動しています。
sentence.reading: けんこう の ために まいにち うんどうしています。
sentence.romaji: kenkou no tame ni mainichi undou shiteimasu
sentence.english: I exercise every day for my health.

fact: The kanji 功 is commonly used to recognize someone's achievements or contributions. Awards, military honors, and public commendations often include words containing 功.
memory: Imagine receiving a gold medal after years of hard work. That achievement helps you remember 功 means 'merit' or 'success.'

sentence.japanese: 彼 の 功績 は 高く 評価されました。
sentence.reading: かれ の こうせき は たかく ひょうかされました。
sentence.romaji: kare no kouseki wa takaku hyouka saremashita
sentence.english: His achievements were highly praised.
